metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: 101 Zen Stories was first published in 1919 by Rider and Company, London, and David McKay Company, Philadelphia.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: These stories recount actual experiences of Chinese and Japanese Zen teachers over a period of more than five centuries.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: The Gateless Gate was first published in 1934 by John Murray, Los Angeles.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: It is a collection of problems, called koan that Zen teachers use in guiding their students toward release, first recorded by a Chinese master in the year 1228.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: 10 Bulls was first published in 1935 by DeVorss and Company, Los Angeles, and subsequently by Ralph R. Phillips, Portland, Oregon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: It is a translation from the Chinese of a famous twelfth century commentary upon the stages of awareness leading to enlightenment and is here illustrated by one of Japan’s best contemporary woodblock artists.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: Centering, a transcription of ancient Sanskrit manuscripts, first appeared in the Spring 1955 issue of Gentry magazine, New York.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: It presents in ancient teaching, still alive in Kashmir and parts of India after more than four thousand years that may well be the roots of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: Thanks are due the publishers named above for permission to gather the material together here.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: And most of all am I grateful to Nyogen Senzaki, ‘homeless monk’ exemplar-friend collaborator, who so delighted with me in transcribing the first three books, even as that prescient man of Kashmir, Lakshmanjoo, did on the fourth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 7

page_content: The first Zen patriarch Bodhidharma brought Zen to China from India in the sixth century.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 7

page_content: According to his biography recorded in the year 1004 by the Chinese teacher Dogen after nine years in China Bodhidharma wished to go home and gathered his disciples about him to test their apperception.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Dofuku said: ‘In my opinion truth is beyond affirmation or negation, for this is the way it moves.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Bodhidharma replied: ‘You have my skin.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: The nun Soji said: ‘In my view, it is like Ananda’s sight of the Buddha- land—seen once and for ever.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Bodhidharma answered: ‘You have my flesh.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Dofuku said: ‘The four elements of light, airiness, fluidity, and solidity are empty (i.e.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 8

page_content: inclusive) and the five skandas are No-things.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 8

page_content: In my opinion,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: No-thing (i.e.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 8

page_content: spirit) is reality.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Bodhidharma commented: ‘You have my bones’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Finally Eka bowed before the master—and remained silent.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Bodhidharma said: ‘You have my marrow.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: Old Zen was so fresh it became treasured and remembered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 16, sentence number 2 on page 8

page_content: Here are fragments of its skin flesh bones but not its marrow—never found in words.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: The directness of Zen has led many to believe it stemmed from sources before the time of Buddha, 500 BC.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 17, sentence number 2 on page 8

page_content: The reader may judge for himself, for he has here for the first time in one book the experiences of Zen, the mind problems, the stages of awareness and a similar teaching predating Zen by centuries.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 18, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: The problem of our mind, relating conscious to preconscious awareness takes us deep into everyday living.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 18, sentence number 2 on page 8

page_content: Dare we open our doors to the source of am being?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 18, sentence number 3 on page 8

page_content: What are flesh and bones for?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: Foreword, paragraph number 19, sentence number 1 on page 8

page_content: PAUL REPS

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 10

page_content: Transcribed by Nyogen Senzaki and Paul Reps

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: These stories were transcribed into English from a book called the Shaseki-shu (Collection of Stone and Sand), written late in the thirteenth century by the Japanese Zen teacher Muju (the ‘non-dweller), and from anecdotes of Zen monks taken from various books published in Japan around the turn of the present century.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: For Orientals, more interested in being than in business the self- discovered man has been the most worthy of respect.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: Such a man proposes to open his consciousness just as the Buddha did.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: These are stories about such self-discoveries.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: The following is adapted from the preface to the first edition of these stories in English.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: Zen might be called the inner art and design of the Orient.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: It was rooted in China by Bodhidharma, who came from India in the sixth century, and was carried eastward into Japan by the twelfth century.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 11

page_content: It has been described as: ‘A special teaching without scriptures, beyond words and letters, pointing to the mind essence of man seeing directly into one’s nature, attaining enlightenment.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: Zen was known as Ch’an in China.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: The Ch’an-Zen masters, instead of being followers of the Buddha, aspire to be his friends and to place themselves in the same responsive relationship with the universe, as did Buddha and Jesus.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 11

page_content: Zen is not a sect but an experience.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: The Zen habit of self-searching through meditation to realize one’s true nature, with disregard of formalism, with insistence on self-discipline and simplicity of living, ultimately won the support of the nobility and ruling classes in Japan and the profound respect of all levels of philosophical thought in the Orient.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: The Noh dramas are Zen stories.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: Zen spirit has come to mean not only peace and understanding, but devotion to art and to work, the rich unfolding of contentment, opening the door to insight, the expression of innate beauty, the intangible charm of incompleteness.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 11

page_content: Zen carries many meanings, none of them, entirely definable.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page 11

page_content: If they a defined they are not Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 11

page_content: It has been said that if you have Zen in your life, you have no fear, no doubt, no unnecessary craving, and no extreme emotion.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 11

page_content: Neither illiberal attitudes nor egotistical actions trouble you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 11

page_content: You serve humanity humbly, fulfilling your presence in this world with loving-kindness and observing your passing as a petal falling from a flower.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 11

page_content: Serene you enjoy life in blissful tranquility.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 12

page_content: Such is the spirit of Zen, whose venture is thousands of temples in China and Japan, priests and monks, wealth and prestige, and often the very formalism it would itself transcend.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 12

page_content: To study Zen, the flowering of one’s nature, is no easy task in any age or civilization.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 12

page_content: Many teachers, true and false, have purposed to assist others in this accomplishment.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 12, sentence number 3 on page 12

page_content: It is from innumerable and actual adventures in Zen that these stories have evolved.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101 ZEN STORIES, paragraph number 12, sentence number 4 on page 12

page_content: May the reader in turn realize them in living experience today.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 13

page_content: Nan-in, a Japanese master during the Meiji era (1868-1912) received a university professor who came to inquire about Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 13

page_content: Nan-in saved tea.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 13

page_content: He poured his visitor’s cup full, and then kept on pouring.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 13

page_content: The professor watched the overflow until he no longer could restrain himself.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 13

page_content: ‘It is overfull.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 13

page_content: No more will go in!’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 13

page_content: ‘Like this cup,’ Nan-in said.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 13

page_content: ‘You are full of your own opinions and speculations.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 13

page_content: How can I show you Zen unless you first empty your cup?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. A Cup of Tea, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 13
Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: Gudo was the emperor’s teacher of his time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: Nevertheless, he used to travel done as a wandering mendicant.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: Once when he was on his way to Edo, the cultural and political center of the shogunate, he approached a little village mad Takenaka.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: It was evening and a heavy rain was falling.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: Gudo was thoroughly wet.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: His straw sandals were in pieces.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 14

page_content: At a farmhouse near the village he noticed four or five pairs of sandals in the window and decided to buy some dry ones.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: The woman who offered him the sandals seeing how wet he was invited him to remain for the night in her home.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: Gudo accepted, thanking her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: He entered and recited a sutra before the family shrine.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 14

page_content: He then was introduced to the woman’s mother, and to her children.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 14

page_content: Observing that the entire family was depressed Gudo asked what was wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: ‘My husband is a gambler and a drunkard,’ the housewife told him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: ‘When he happens to win he drinks and becomes abusive.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: When he losses he borrows money from others.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 14

page_content: Sometimes when he becomes thoroughly drunk he does not come home at all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 14

page_content: What can I do?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: ‘I will help him,’ said Gudo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: ‘Here is some money.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: Get me a gallon of fine wine and something good to eat.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 14

page_content: Then you may retire.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 14

page_content: I will meditate before the shrine.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: When the man of the house returned about midnight, quite drunk; he bellowed: ‘Hey, wife I am home.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: Have you something for me eat?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: I have something for you: said Gudo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: ‘I happened to be caught in the rain and your wife kindly asked me to remain here for the night.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: In return I have bought some wine and fish.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 14

page_content: You might as well have them.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: The man was delighted.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: He drank the wine at once and laid himself down on the floor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: Gudo sat in mediation beside him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: In the morning when the husband awoke he had forgotten about the previous night.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 14

page_content: ‘Who are you?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 14

page_content: Where do yon come from?’ he asked Gudo, who still was meditating.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 14

page_content: ‘I am Gudo of Kyoto and I am going on to Edo,’ replied the Zen master. The man was utterly ashamed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: He apologized profusely to the teacher of his emperor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: Gudo smiled.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: ‘Everything in this life is impermanent’ he explained.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 15

page_content: ‘Life is very brief.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 15

page_content: If you keep on gambling and drinking yon will have no time left to accomplish anything else, and you will cause your family to suffer too.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: The perception of the husband awoke as if from a dream.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: ‘You are right,’ he declared.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 12, sentence number 3 on page 15

page_content: ‘How can I ever repay you for this wonderful teaching!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 12, sentence number 4 on page 15

page_content: Let me see you off and carry your things a little way.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: ‘If you wish,’ assented Gudo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: The two started out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 13, sentence number 3 on page 15

page_content: After they had gone three miles Gudo told him to return.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 13, sentence number 4 on page 15

page_content: ‘Just another five miles,’ he begged Gudo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 13, sentence number 5 on page 15

page_content: They continued on.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: You may return now,’ suggested Gudo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: ‘After another ten miles,’ the man replied.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: ‘Return now,’ said Gudo, when the ten miles had been passed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: ‘I am going to follow you all the rest of my life,’ declared the man.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 18, sentence number 1 on page 15

page_content: Modern Zen teachers in Japan spring from the lineage of a famous master who was the successor of Gudo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Finding a Diamond on a Muddy Road, paragraph number 18, sentence number 2 on page 15

page_content: His name was Mu-nan, the man who never returned back.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: The Zen master Hakuin was praised by his neighbors as one living a pure life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: A beautiful Japanese girl whose parents owned a food store lived near him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: Suddenly, without any warning her parents discovered she was with child.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: This made her parents angry.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: She would not confess who the man was, but after much harassment at last named Hakuin.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: In great anger the parents went to the master.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: ‘Is that so?’ was all he would say.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: After the child was born it was brought to Hakuin.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: By this time he had lost his reputation, which did not trouble him, but he took very good care of the child.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 16

page_content: He obtained milk from his neighbors and everything else the little one needed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: A year later the girl-mother could stand it no longer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: She told her parents the truth—that the real father of the child was a young man who worked in the fish market.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: The mother and father of the girl at once went to Hakuin to ask his forgiveness, to apologize at length, and to get the child back again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 16

page_content: Hakuin was willing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Is That So?, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 16

page_content: In yielding the child, all he said was, ‘Is that so?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: The master Bankei’s talks were attended not only by Zen students but by persons of all ranks and sects.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: He never quoted sutras nor indulged in scholastic dissertations.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 17

page_content: Instead his words were spoken directly from his heart to the harts of his listeners.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: His large audiences angered a priest of the Nichiren sect because the adherents had left to hear about Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: The self-centered Nichiren priest came to the temple determined to debate with Bankei.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: ‘Hey, Zen teacher,’ he called out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: ‘Wait a minute.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 17

page_content: Whoever respects you will obey what you say, but a man like myself does not respect you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 17

page_content: Can you make me obey yon?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: ‘Come up beside me and I will show you,’ said Bankei.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: Proudly the priest pushed his way through the crowd to the teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: Bankei smiled.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: ‘Come over to my left side.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: The priest obeyed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: ‘No,’ said Bankei, ‘we may talk better if you are on the right side.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: Step over here.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: The priest proudly stepped over to the right.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 17

page_content: ‘You see,’ observed Bankei, ‘you are obeying me and I think you are a very gentle person.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Obedience, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 17

page_content: Now sit down and listen.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 18

page_content: Twenty monks and one nun, who was named Eshun, were practicing meditation with a certain Zen master.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 18

page_content: Eshun was very pretty even though her head was shaved and her dress plain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 18

page_content: Several monks secretly fell in love with her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 18

page_content: One of them wrote her a love letter, insisting upon a private meeting.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 18

page_content: Eshun did not reply.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 18

page_content: The following day the master gave a lecture to the group, and when it was over, Eshun arose.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. If You Love, Love Openly, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 18

page_content: Addressing the one who had written her, she said: ‘If you really love me so much, come and embrace me now.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: There was an old woman in China who had supported a monk for over twenty years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 19

page_content: She had built a little hut for him and fed him while he was meditating.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 19

page_content: Finally she wondered just what progress he had made in all this time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: To find out, she obtained the help of a girl rich in desire.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 19

page_content: ‘Go and embrace him,’ she told her;’ and then ask him suddenly:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: “What now?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: The girl called upon the monk and without much ado caressed him, asking him what he was going to do about it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: ‘An old tree grows on a cold rock in winter,’ replied the monk somewhat poetically.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 19

page_content: ‘Nowhere is there any warmth.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: The girl returned and related what he had said.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: ‘To think I fed that fellow for twenty years!’ exclaimed the old woman in anger.’ He showed no consideration for your need, no disposition to explain your condition.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 19

page_content: He need not have responded to passion, but at last he should have evidenced some compassion.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. No Loving-Kindness, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 19

page_content: She at once went to the hut of the monk and burned it down.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 20

page_content: Tanzan wrote sixty postal cards on the last day of his life, and asked an attendant to mail them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 20

page_content: Then he passed away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 20

page_content: The cards read:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 20

page_content: I am departing from this world.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 20

page_content: This is my last announcement.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 20

page_content: Tanzan

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Announcement, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 20
Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: In the early days of the Meiji era there lived a well-known wrestler called O-nami, Great Waves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: O-nami was immensely strong and knew the art of wrestling.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: In his private bouts he defeated even his teacher, but in public he was so bashful that his own pupils threw him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: O-nami felt he should go to a Zen master for help.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: Hakuju, a wandering teacher, was stopping in a little temple nearby, so O-nami went to see him and told him of his trouble.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: ‘Great Waves is your name,’ the teacher advised,’ so stay in this temple tonight.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: Imagine that you are those billows.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 21

page_content: You are no longer a wrestler who is afraid.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 21

page_content: You are those huge waves sweeping everything before them, swallowing all in their path.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 21

page_content: Do this and you will be the greatest wrestler in the land.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: The teacher retired.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: O-nami sat in meditation trying to imagine himself as waves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 21

page_content: He thought of many different things.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: Then gradually he turned more and more to the feelings of the waves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: As the night advanced the waves became larger and larger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 21

page_content: They swept away the flowers in their vases.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 21

page_content: Even the Buddha in the shrine was inundated.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 21

page_content: Before dawn the temple was nothing but the ebb and flow of an immense sea.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: In the morning the teacher found O-nami meditating, a faint smile on his face.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: He patted the wrestlers shoulder.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 21

page_content: ‘Now nothing can disturb you.’ he said.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 21

page_content: ‘You are the waves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 21

page_content: You will sweep everything before you.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 21

page_content: The same day O-nami entered the wrestling contests and won.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Great Waves, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 21

page_content: After that, no one in Japan was able to defeat him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 22

page_content: Ryokan, a Zen master, lived the simplest kind of life in a little hut at the foot of a mountain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 22

page_content: One evening a thief visited the hut only to discover there was nothing in it to stea1.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 22

page_content: Ryokan returned and caught him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 22

page_content: ‘You may have come a long way to visit me,’ he told the prowler, ‘and you should not return empty-handed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 22

page_content: Please take my clothes as a gift.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 22

page_content: The thief was bewildered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 22

page_content: He took the clothes and slunk away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 22

page_content: Ryokan sat naked, watching the moon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. The Moon cannot be Stolen, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 22

page_content: ‘Poor fellow,’ he mused, ‘I wish I could give him this beautiful moon.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: The Zen master Hoshin lived in China many years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: Then he returned to the northeastern part of Japan, where he taught his disciples.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 23

page_content: When he was getting very old, he told them a story he had heard in China.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: This is the story:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: One year on the twenty-fifth of December, Tokufu, who was very old, said to his disciples: I am not going to-be alive next year so you fellows should treat me well this year.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: The pupils thought he was joking, but since he was a great-hearted teacher each of them in turn treated him to a feast on succeeding days of the departing year.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: On the eve of the New Year, Tokufu concluded: ‘You have been good to me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: I shall leave you tomorrow afternoon when the snow has stopped.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: The disciples laughed, thinking he was aging and talking nonsense since the night was clear and without snow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: But at midnight snow began to fall, and the next day they did not find their teacher about.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 23

page_content: They went to the meditation hall.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: There he had passed on.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: Hoshin, who related this story, told his disciples: ‘It is not necessary for a Zen master to predict his passing, but if he really wishes to do so, he can.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: ‘Can you?’ someone asked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: ‘Yes,’ answered Hoshin.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: ‘I will show you what I can do seven days from now.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: None of the disciple’s believed him, and most of them had even forgotten the conversation when Hoshin next called them together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: ‘Seven days ago,’ he remarked, ‘I said I was going to leave you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 23

page_content: It is customary to write a farewell poem, but I am neither poet nor calligrapher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 12, sentence number 3 on page 23

page_content: Let one of you inscribe my last words.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: His followers thought he was joking, but one of them started to write.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: ‘Are you ready?’ Hoshin asked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: ‘Yes, sir,’ replied the writer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: Then Hoshin dictated:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 23

page_content: I came from brilliancy And return to brilliancy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 18, sentence number 1 on page 24

page_content: What is this?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 19, sentence number 1 on page 24

page_content: The poem was one line short of the customary four, so, the disciple said: ‘Master, we are one line short.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. The Last Poem of Hoshin, paragraph number 20, sentence number 1 on page 24

page_content: Hoshin, with the roar of a conquering lion, shouted ‘Kaa!’ and was gone.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: The exquisite Shunkai whose other name was Suzu was compelled to marry against her wishes when she was quite young.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: Later, after this marriage had ended, she attended the university, where she studied philosophy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: To see Shunkai was to fall in love with her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: Moreover, wherever she went, she herself fell in love with others.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: Love was with her at the university, and afterwards, when philosophy did not satisfy her and she visited a temple to learn about Zen, the Zen students fell in love with her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 25

page_content: Shunkai’s whole life was saturated with love.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: At last in Kyoto she became a real student of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: Her brothers in the sub-temple of Kennin praised her sincerity.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: One of them proved to be a congenial spirit and assisted her in the mastery of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: The abbot of Kennin, Mokurai, Silent Thunder, was severe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: He kept the precepts himself and expected his priests to do so.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: In modern Japan whatever zeal these priests have lost for Buddhism they seem to have gained for having wives.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: Mokurai used to take a broom and chase the women away when he found them in any of his temples, but the more wives he swept out, the more seemed to come back.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: In this particular temple the wife of the head priest became jealous of Shunkai’s earnestness and beauty.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: Hearing the students praise her serious Zen made this wife squirm and itch.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: Finally she spread a rumor about Shunkai and the young man who was now her friend.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 25

page_content: As a consequence he was expelled and Shunkai was removed from the temple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: ‘I may have made the mistake of love,’ thought Shunkai, ‘but the priest’s wife shall not remain in the temple either if my friend is to be treated so unjustly.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: Shunkai the same night with a can of kerosene set fire to the five hundred year old temple and burned it to the ground.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: In the morning she found herself in the hands of the police.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 25

page_content: A young lawyer became interested in her and endeavored to make her sentence lighter.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 25

page_content: ‘Do not help me,’ she told him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 25

page_content: I might decide to do something else which would only imprison me again.’ At last a sentence of seven years was completed, and Shunkai was released from the prison, where the sixty-three-year-old warden also had become enamored of her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 26

page_content: But now everyone looked upon her as a ‘jailbird’.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 26

page_content: No one would associate with her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 26

page_content: Even the Zen people, who are supposed to believe in enlightenment in this life and with this body, shunned her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page 26

page_content: Zen, Shunkai found, was one thing and the followers of Zen quite another.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 10, sentence number 5 on page 26

page_content: Her relatives would have nothing to do with her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 10, sentence number 6 on page 26

page_content: She grew sick, poor, and weak.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 26

page_content: She met a Shinshu priest who taught her the name of the Buddha of Love, and in this Shunkai found some solace and peace of mind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 26

page_content: She passed away when she was still exquisitely beautiful and hardly thirty years old.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 26

page_content: She wrote her own story in a futile endeavor to support herself and some of it she told to a woman writer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 26

page_content: So it reached the Japanese people.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. The Story of Shunkai, paragraph number 12, sentence number 3 on page 26

page_content: Those who rejected Shunkai, those who slandered and hated her, now read of her life with tears of remorse.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: Anyone walking about Chinatowns in America will observe statues of a stout fellow carrying a linen sack.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 27

page_content: Chinese merchants call him Happy Chinaman or Laughing Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: This Hotei lived in the T’ang dynasty.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 27

page_content: He had no desire to call himself a Zen master or to gather many disciples about him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 27

page_content: Instead he walked the streets with a big sack into which he would put gifts of candy, fruit, or doughnuts.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 27

page_content: These he would give to children who gathered around him in play.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 27

page_content: He established a kindergarten of the streets.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: Whenever he met a Zen devotee he would extend his hand and say: ‘Give me one penny.’ And if anyone asked him to return to a temple to teach others, again he would reply: ‘Give me one penny.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: Once as he was about his play work another Zen master happened along and inquired: ‘What is the significance of Zen?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: Hotei immediately plopped his sack down on the ground in silent answer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: ‘Then,’ asked the other, ‘what is the actualization of Zen?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Happy Chinaman, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 27

page_content: At once the Happy Chinaman swung the sack over his shoulder and continued on his way.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: In Tokyo in the Meiji era there lived two prominent teachers of opposite characteristics.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 28

page_content: One, Unsho, an instructor in Shingon, kept Buddha’s precepts scrupulously.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 28

page_content: He never drank intoxicants, nor did he eat after eleven o’clock in the morning.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: The other teacher, Tanzan, a professor of philosophy at the Imperial University, never observed the precepts.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 28

page_content: When he felt like eating he ate, and when he felt like sleeping in the daytime he slept.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: One day Unsho visited Tanzan, who was drinking wine at the time, not even a drop of which is supposed to touch the tongue of a Buddhist.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: ‘Hello, brother,’ Tanzan greeted him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 28

page_content: ‘Won’t you have a drink?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: ‘I never drink!’ exclaimed Unsho solemnly.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: ‘One who does not drink is not even human,’ said Tanzan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: ‘Do you mean to call me inhuman just because I do not indulge in intoxicating liquids!’ exclaimed Unsho in anger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 28

page_content: Then if I am not human, what am I?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. A Buddha, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 28

page_content: ‘A Buddha.’ answered Tanzan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: Tanzan and Ekido were once traveling together down a muddy road.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: A heavy rain was still falling.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: Coming around a bend, they met a lovely girl in a silk kimono and sash, unable to cross the intersection.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: ‘Come on, girl,’ said Tanzan at once.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: Lifting her in his arms, he carried her over the mud.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: Ekido did not speak again until that night when they reached a lodging temple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: Then he no longer could restrain himself.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: ‘We monks don’t go near females.’ He told Tanzan, especially not young and lovely ones.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: It is dangerous.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 29

page_content: Why did you do that?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 29

page_content: ‘I left the girl there,’ said Tanzan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Muddy Road, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 29

page_content: ‘Are you still carrying her?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: Shoun became a teacher of Soto Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: When he was still a student his father passed away, leaving him to care for his old mother.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: Whenever Shoun went to a meditation hall he always took his mother with him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: Since she accompanied him, when he visited monasteries he could not live with the monks.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: So he would build a little house and care for her there.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 30

page_content: He would copy sutras, Buddhist verses and in this manner receive a few coins for food.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: When Shoun bought fish for his mother, the people would scoff at him, for a monk is not supposed to eat fish.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: But Shoun did not mind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: His mother, however, was hurt to see others laugh at her son.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 30

page_content: Finally she told Shoun: ‘I think I will become a nun.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 30

page_content: I can be a vegetarian too.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: She did and they studied together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: Shoun was fond of music and was a master of the harp, which his mother also played.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: On full-moon nights they used to play together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: One night a young lady passed by their house and heard music.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: Deeply touched, she invited Shoun to visit her the next evening and play.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: He accepted the invitation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 30

page_content: A few days later he met the young lady on the street and thanked her for her hospitality.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 6, sentence number 5 on page 30

page_content: Others laughed at him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 6, sentence number 6 on page 30

page_content: He had visited the home of a woman of the streets.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: One day Shoun left a distant temple to deliver a lecture.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: A few months afterwards he returned home to find his mother dead.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: Friends had not known where to reach him, so the funeral was then in progress.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: Shoun walked up and hit the coffin with his staff.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: ‘Mother, your son has returned,’ he said.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: ‘I am glad to see you have returned son,’ he answered for his mother.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: ‘I’m glad too,’ Shoun responded.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: Then he announced to the people about him: The funeral ceremony is over.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: You may bury the body.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: When Shoun was old he knew his end was approaching.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 30

page_content: He asked his disciples to gather around him in the morning telling them he was going to pass on at noon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 30

page_content: Burning incense before the picture of his mother and his old teacher, he wrote a poem:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 30

page_content: For fifty-six years I lived as best I could, Making my way in this world.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 31

page_content: Now the rain has ended, the clouds are clearing,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 31

page_content: The blue sky has a full moon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Shoun and His Mother, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 31

page_content: His disciples gathered about him, reciting a sutra, and Shoun passed on during the invocation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 32

page_content: A university student while visiting Gasan asked him: ‘Have you ever read the Christian Bible?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 32

page_content: ‘No, read it to me,’ said Gasan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 32

page_content: The student opened the Bible and read from St Mattew: ‘And why take ye thought for raiment?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 32

page_content: Consider the lilies of the field, how they grow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 32

page_content: They toil not, neither do they spin, and yet I say unto you that even Solomon in all his glory was not arrayed like one of these.
