metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 32

page_content: …Take therefore no thought for the morrow, for the morrow shall take thought for the things of itself.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 32

page_content: Gasan said: ‘Whoever uttered those words I consider an enlightened man.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 32

page_content: The student continued reading: ‘Ask and it shall be given you, seek and ye shall find, knock and it shall be opened unto you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 32

page_content: For everyone that asketh receiveth, and he that seeketh findeth, and to him that knocketh, it shall be opened.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 32

page_content: Gasan remarked: ‘That is excellent.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Not Far from Buddahood, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 32

page_content: Whoever said that is not far from Buddhahood.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: A young physician in Tokyo named Kusuda met a college friend who had been studying Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: The young doctor asked him what Zen was.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: ‘I cannot tell you what it is,’ the friend replied, ‘but one thing is certain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: If you understand Zen, you will not be afraid to die.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: That’s fine: said Kusuda.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: “I will try it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: Where can I find a teacher?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: ‘Go to the master Nan-in,’ the friend told him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: So Kusuda went to call on Nan-in.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: He carried a dagger nine and a half inches long to determine whether or not the teacher himself was afraid to die.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: When Nan-in saw Kusuda he exclaimed: ‘Hello, friend.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: How are you?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: We haven’t seen each other for a long time!’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: This perplexed Kusuda, who replied: ‘We have never met before.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: ‘That’s right,’ answered Nan-in.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: I mistook you for another physician who is receiving instruction here.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: With such a beginning, Kusuda lost his chance to test the master, so reluctantly be asked if he might receive Zen instruction.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: Nan-in said: ‘Zen is not a difficult task.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: If you are a physician, treat your patients with kindness.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: That is Zen.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: Kusuda visited Nan-in three times.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: Each time Nan-in told him the same thing.’ A physician should not waste time around here.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: Go home and take care of your patients.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: It was not yet clear to Kusuda how such teaching could remove the fear of death.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: So on his fourth visit he complained:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: ‘My friend told me when one learns Zen one loses his fear of death.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: Each time I come here all you tell me is to take care of my patients.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 13, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: I know that much.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 13, sentence number 4 on page 33

page_content: If that is your so-called Zen, I am not going to visit you any more.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 33

page_content: Nan-in smiled and patted the doctor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 33

page_content: ‘I have been too strict with you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 14, sentence number 3 on page 33

page_content: Let me give you a koan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 14, sentence number 4 on page 33

page_content: ‘ He presented Kusuda with Joshu’s Mu to work over, which is the first mind-enlightening problem in the book called The Gateless Gate. Kusuda pondered this problem of Mu (No-thing) for two years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 15, sentence number 2 on page 34

page_content: At length he thought he had reached certainty of mind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 34

page_content: But his teacher commented: “You are not in yet.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 34

page_content: Kusuda continued in concentration for another year and a half.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 16, sentence number 2 on page 34

page_content: His mind became placid.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 16, sentence number 3 on page 34

page_content: Problems dissolved.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 16, sentence number 4 on page 34

page_content: No-thing became the truth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 16, sentence number 5 on page 34

page_content: He served his patients well and, without even knowing it, be was free from concern over life and death.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. Stingy in Teaching, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 34

page_content: Then when he visited Nan-in his old teacher just smiled.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 35

page_content: Buddha told a parable in a sutra: A man traveling across a field encountered a tiger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 35

page_content: He fled, the tiger after him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 35

page_content: Coming to a precipice, he caught hold of the root of a wild vine and swung himself down over the edge.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 35

page_content: The tiger sniffed at him from above.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 35

page_content: Trembling, the man looked down to where, far below, mother tiger was waiting to eat him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 35

page_content: Only the vine sustained him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 35

page_content: Two mice one white and one black, little by little started to gnaw away the vine.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 35

page_content: The man saw a luscious strawberry near him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 35

page_content: Grasping the vine with one hand, he plucked the strawberry with the other.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. A Parable, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 35

page_content: How sweet it tasted!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: When one goes to Obaku temple in Kyoto one sees carved over the gate the words ‘ The First principle ‘.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 36

page_content: The letters are unusually large, and those who appreciate calligraphy always admire them as being a masterpiece.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 36

page_content: They were drawn by Kosen two hundred years ago.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: When the master drew them he did so on paper, from which workmen made the larger carving in wood.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 36

page_content: As Kosen sketched the letters a bold pupil was with him who had made several gallons of ink for the calligraphy and who never failed to criticize his master’s work.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: That is not good,’ he told Kosen after the first effort.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: ‘How is that one?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: ‘Poor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 36

page_content: Worse than before,’ pronounced the pupil.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: Kosen patiently wrote one sheet after another until eighty-four First Principles had accumulated, still without the approval of the pupil.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: Then, when the young man stepped outside for a few moments, Kosen thought: ‘Now is my chance to escape his keen eye,’ and he wrote hurriedly, with a mind free from distraction: ‘The First principle.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. The First Principle, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 36

page_content: ‘A masterpiece,’ pronounced the pupil.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 37

page_content: Jiun, a Shingon master, war a well-known Sanskrit scholar of the Tokugawa era.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 37

page_content: When he was young he used to deliver lectures to his brother students.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 37

page_content: His mother heard about this and wrote him a letter:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 37

page_content: ‘Son, I do not think you become a devotee of the Buddha because you desire to turn into a walking dictionary for others.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 37

page_content: There is no end to information and commentation, glory and honor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 37

page_content: I wish you would stop this lecture business.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 37

page_content: Shut yourself up in a little temple in a remote part of the mountain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. A Mother’s Advice, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 37

page_content: Devote your time to meditation and in this way attain hue realization.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: The masts of Kennin temple was Mokurai, Silent Thunder.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: He had a little protégé named Toyo who was only twelve years old.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: Toyo saw the olds disciples visit the master’s room each morning and evening to receive instruction in sanzen or personal guidance in which they were given koans to stop mind- wandering.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: Toro wished to do sanzen also.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: ‘Wait a while,’ said Mokurai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: ‘You are too young.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: But the child insisted, so the teacher finally consented.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: In the evening little Toyo went at the props time to the threshold of Mokurai’s sanzen room.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: He struck the gong to announce his presence, bowed respectfully three times outside the door, and went to sit before the master in respectful silence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: ‘You can hear the sound of two hands when they clap together,’ said Mokurai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: ‘Now show me the sound of one hand.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: Toyo bowed and went to his room to consider this problem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: From his window he could hear the music of the geishas.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: ‘Ah, I have it!’ he proclaimed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: The next evening, when his teacher asked him to illustrate the sound of one hand, Toyo began to play the music of the geishas.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: ‘No, no,’ said Mokurai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: That will never do.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: That is not the sound of one hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 10, sentence number 4 on page 38

page_content: You’ve not got it at all.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: Thinking that such music might interrupt, Toyo moved his abode to a quiet place.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: He meditated again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: ‘What can the sound of one hand be?’ He happened to hear some water dripping.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 38

page_content: ‘I have it,’ imagined Toyo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: When he next appeared before his teacher, Toyo imitated dripping water.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: ‘What is that?’ asked Mokurai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: That is the sound of dripping water, but not the sound of one hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 13, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: Try again.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: In vain Toyo meditated to hear the sound of one hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: He heard the sighing of the wind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 14, sentence number 3 on page 38

page_content: But the sound was rejected.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: He heard the cry of an owl.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 15, sentence number 2 on page 38

page_content: This also was refused.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 38

page_content: The sound of one hand was not the locusts.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 39

page_content: For more than ten times Toyo visited Mokurai with different sounds.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 17, sentence number 2 on page 39

page_content: All were wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 17, sentence number 3 on page 39

page_content: For almost a year he pondered what the sound of one hand might be.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 18, sentence number 1 on page 39

page_content: At last little Toyo entered true meditation and transcended all sounds.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 18, sentence number 2 on page 39

page_content: ‘I could collect no more,’ he explained later.’ so I reached the soundless sound.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. The Sound of One Hand, paragraph number 19, sentence number 1 on page 39

page_content: Toyo had realized the sound of one hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Soyen Shaku, the first Zen teacher to come to America, said: ‘My heart burns like fire but my eyes are as cold as dead ashes.’ He made the following rules, which he practiced every day of his life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: In the morning before dressing, light incense and meditate.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Retire at a regular hour.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 40

page_content: Partake of food at regular intervals.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 40

page_content: Eat with moderation and never to the point of satisfaction.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Receive a guest with the same attitude you have when alone.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 40

page_content: When alone, maintain the same attitude you have in receiving guests.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Watch what you say, and whatever you say, practice it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: When an opportunity comes do not let it pass by, yet always think twice before acting.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Do not regret the past.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 40

page_content: Look to the future.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Have the fearless attitude of a hero and the loving heart of a child.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 40

page_content: Upon retiring sleep as if you had entered your last sleep.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. My Heart Burns Like Fire, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 40

page_content: Upon awakening, leave your bed behind you instantly as if you had cast away a pair of old shoes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Eshun’s Departure, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: When Eshun, the Zennun, was past sixty and about to leave the world, she asked some monks to pile up wood in the yard.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Eshun’s Departure, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: Seating herself firmly in the center of the funeral pyre, she had it set fire around the edges.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Eshun’s Departure, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: ‘O nun!’ shouted the monk, ‘is it hot in there?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Eshun’s Departure, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: ‘Such a matter would concern only a stupid person like yourself,’ answered Eshun.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Eshun’s Departure, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 41

page_content: The flames arose, and she passed away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: A farmer requested a Tendai priest to recite sutras for his wife, who had died.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 42

page_content: After the recitation was over the farmer asked: ‘Do you think my wife will gain merit from this?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: ‘Not only your wife but all sentient beings will benefit from the recitation of sutras,’ answered the priest.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: ‘If you say all sentient beings will benefit,’ said the farmer, ‘my wife may be very weak and others will take advantage of her, getting the benefit she should have.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 42

page_content: So please recite sutras just for her.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: The priest explained that it was the desire of a Buddhist to offer blessings and wish merit for every living being.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 42

page_content: That is a fine teaching,’ concluded the farmer, ‘but please make one exception.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 42

page_content: I have a neighbor who is rough and mean to me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Reciting Sutras, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 42

page_content: Just exclude him from all those sentient beings.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: Suiwo, the disciple of Hakuin, was a good teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: During one summer seclusion period, a pupil came to him from a southern island of Japan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: Suiwo gave him the problem: ‘Hear the sound of one hand.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: The pupil remained three years but could not pass this test.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: One night he came in tears to Suiwo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: ‘I must return south in shame and embarrassment,’ he said, ‘for I cannot solve my problem.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: ‘Wait one week more and meditate constantly,’ advised Suiwo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: Still no enlightenment came to the pupil.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: Try for another week,’ said Suiwo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 43

page_content: The pupil obeyed, but in vain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: ‘Still another week.’ Yet this was of no avail.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 43

page_content: In despair the student begged to be released, but Suiwo requested another meditation of five day.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 43

page_content: They were without result.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 43

page_content: Then he said: ‘Mediate for three day longer, then if you fail to attain enlightenment, you had better kill yourself.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Three Days More, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 43

page_content: On the second day the pupil was enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: Provided he makes and wins an argument about Buddhism with those who live there any wandering monk can remain in a Zen temple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: If he is defeated, he has to move on.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: In a temple in the northern part of Japan two brothers monks were dwelling together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: The elder one was learned, but the younger one was stupid and had but one eye.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: A wandering monk came and asked for lodging properly challenging them to a debate about the sublime teaching.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: The elder brother, tired that day from much studying, told the younger one to take his place.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: ‘Go and request the dialogue in silence,’ he cautioned.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: So the young monk and the strange went to the shrine and sat down.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: Shortly afterwards the traveler rose and went in to the elder brother and said: ‘Your young brother is a wonderful fellow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: He defeated me.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: ‘Relate the dialogue to me,’ said the elder one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: ‘Well,’ explained the traveler,’ first I held up one finger, representing Buddha, the enlightened one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: So he held up two fingers, signifying Buddha and his teaching.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: I held up three fingers representing Buddha, his teaching, and his followers living the harmonious life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 44

page_content: Then he shook his clenched fist in my face, indicating that all three come from one realization.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: Thus he won and so I have no right to remain here.’ With this, the traveler left.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: ‘Where is that fellow?’ asked the younger one, running in to his elder brother.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: “I understand you won the debate.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: ‘Won nothing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: I’m going to beat him up.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: ‘Tell me the subject of the debate,’ asked the elder one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 44

page_content: ‘Why, the minute he saw me he held up one finger, insulting me by insinuating that I have only one eye.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 44

page_content: Since he was a stranger I thought I would be polite to him, so I held up two fingers congratulating him that he her two eyes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 12, sentence number 3 on page 44

page_content: Then the impolite wretch held up three fingers, suggesting that between us we only have three eyes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Trading Dialogue for Lodging, paragraph number 12, sentence number 4 on page 44

page_content: So I got mad and started to punch him, bur he ran out and that ended it.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. The Voice of Happiness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 46

page_content: After Bankei had passed away, a blind man who lived near the master’s temple told a friend: ‘Since I am blind, I cannot watch a person’s face, so I must judge his character by the sound of his voice.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. The Voice of Happiness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 46

page_content: Ordinarily when I hear someone congratulate another upon his happiness or success, I also hear a secret tone of envy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. The Voice of Happiness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 46

page_content: When condolence is expressed for the misfortune of another.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. The Voice of Happiness, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 46

page_content: I hear pleasure and satisfaction as if the one condoling was really glad there was something left to gain in his own world.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. The Voice of Happiness, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 46

page_content: ‘In all my experience however, Bankei’s voice was always sincere.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. The Voice of Happiness, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 46

page_content: Whenever he expressed happiness, I heard nothing but happiness, and whenever he expressed sorrow, sorrow was all I heard.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: Daiju visited the master Baso in China.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 47

page_content: Baso asked: ‘What do you seek?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: ‘Enlightenment,’ replied Daiju.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: ‘You have your own treasure house.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 47

page_content: Why do you search outside?’ Baso asked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: Daiju inquired : ‘Where is my treasure house?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: Baso answered: ‘What you me asking me is your treasure house.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: Daiju was enlightened!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 47

page_content: Ever after he urged his friends:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Open Your Own Treasure House, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 47

page_content: ‘Open your own treasure house and use those treasures.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: When the nun Chiyono studied Zen under Bukko of Engarku she was unable to attain the fruits of meditation for a long time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: At last one moonlit night she was carrying water in an old pail bound with bamboo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 48

page_content: The bamboo broke and the bottom fell out of the pail, and at that moment Chiyono was set free!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: In commemoration, she wrote a poem:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: In this way and that I tried to save the old pail

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: Since the bamboo strip was weakening and about to break

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: Until at last the bottom fell out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: No more water in the Pail!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. No Water, No Moon, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 48

page_content: No more moon in the water!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: Keichu, the great Zen teacher of the Meiji era, was the head of Tofuku, a cathedral in Kyoto.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: One day the governor of Kyoto called upon him for the first time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: His attendant presented the card of the governor, which read: Kitagaki, Governor of Kyoto.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: ‘I have no business with such a fellow.’ said Keichu to his attendant.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: Tell him to get out of here.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: The attendant carried the card back with apologies.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: ‘That was my error,’ said the governor and with a pencil he scratched out the words Governor of Kyoto.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 49

page_content: ‘Ask your teacher again.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 49

page_content: ‘Oh, is that Kitagaki?’ exclaimed the teacher when he saw the card.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. Calling Card, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 49

page_content: ‘I want to see that fellow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Everything is Best, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 50

page_content: When Banzan was walking through a market he overheard a conversation between a butcher and his customer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Everything is Best, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 50

page_content: ‘Give me the best piece of meat you have,’ said the customer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Everything is Best, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 50

page_content: ‘Everything in my shop is the best,’ replied me butcher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Everything is Best, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 50

page_content: “You cannot find here any piece of meat that is not the best.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Everything is Best, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 50

page_content: At these words Banzan became enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: A lord asked Takuan, a Zen teacher to suggest how he might pass the time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 51

page_content: He felt his day very long attending his office and sitting stiffly to receive the homage of others.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: Takuan wrote eight Chinese characters and gave them to the man:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: Not twice this day

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: Inch time foot gem

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: This day will not come again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. Inch Time Foot Gem, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 51

page_content: Each minute is worth a priceless gem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: Mokusen Hiki was living in a temple in the province of Tamba.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 52

page_content: One of his adherents complained of the stinginess of his wife.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 52

page_content: Mokusen visited the adherent’s wife and showed her his clenched fist before her face.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: ‘What do you mean by that?’ asked the surprised woman.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: ‘Suppose my fist were always like that.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 52

page_content: What would you call it?’ he asked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: ‘Deformed,’ replied the woman.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: Then he opened his hand flat in her face and asked: ‘Suppose it were always like that.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 52

page_content: What then?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: ‘Another kind of deformity,’ said the wife.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: ‘If you understand that much,’ finished Mokusen, ‘you are a good wife.’ Then he left.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. Mokusen’s Hand, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 52

page_content: After his visit, this wife helped her husband to distribute as well as to save.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: Mokugen was never known to smile until his last day on earth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: When his time came to pass away he said to his faithful ones: ‘You have studied under me for more than ten years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 53

page_content: Show me your real interpretation of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 53

page_content: Whoever expresses this most clearly shall be my successor and receive my robe and bowl.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: Everyone watched Mokugen’s severe face but no one answered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: Encho, a disciple who bad been with his teacher for a long time, moved near the bedside.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: He pushed forward the medicine cup a few inches.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 53

page_content: This was his answer to the command.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: The teacher’s face became even more severe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: ‘Is that all you understand?’ he asked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 53

page_content: Encho reached out and moved the cup back again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 53

page_content: A beautiful smile broke ova the features of Mokugen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 53

page_content: ‘You rascal,’ he told Encho.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 53

page_content: ‘You worked with me ten years and have not yet seen my whole body.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 53

page_content: Take the robe and bowl.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. A Smile in His Lifetime, paragraph number 5, sentence number 6 on page 53

page_content: They belong to you.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 54

page_content: Zen students are with their masters at last ten years before they presume to teach others.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 54

page_content: Nan-in was visited by Tenno, who, having passed his apprenticeship, had become a teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 54

page_content: The day happened to be rainy, so Tenno wore wooden clogs and carried an umbrella.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 54

page_content: After greeting him Nan- in remarked: ‘I suppose you left your wooden clogs in the vestibule.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 54

page_content: I want to know if your umbrella is on the right or left side of the clogs.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 54

page_content: Tenno, confused, had no instant answer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 54

page_content: He realized that he was unable to carry his Zen every minute.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Every-Minute Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 54

page_content: He became Nan-in’s pupil, and he studied six more years to accomplish his every-minute Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: Subhuti was Buddha’s disciple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 55

page_content: He was able to understand the potency of emptiness, the viewpoint that nothing exists accept in its relationship of subjectivity and objectivity.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: One day Subhuti, in a mood of sublime emptiness was sitting under a tree.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 55

page_content: Flowers began to fall about him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: ‘We are praising you for your discourse on emptiness,’ the gods whispered to him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: ‘But I have not spoken of emptiness,’ said Subhuti.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 55

page_content: ‘You have not spoken of emptiness, we have not heard emptiness,’ responded the gods.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Flower Shower, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 55

page_content: This is the true emptiness.’ And blossoms showered upon Subhuti as rain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: Tetsugen, a devotee of Zen in Japan, decided to publish the sutras, which at that time were available only in Chinese.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: The books were to be printed with wood block in an edition of seven thousand copies, a tremendous undertaking.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: Tetsugen began by traveling and collecting donations for this purpose.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: A few sympathizers would give him a hundred pieces of gold, but most of the time he received only small coins.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 56

page_content: He thanked each donor with equal gratitude.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 56

page_content: After ten yens Tetsugen had enough money to begin his task.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: It happened that it that time the Uji River overflowed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: Famine followed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 56

page_content: Tetsugen took the funds he had collected for the book and spent them to save others from starvation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 56

page_content: Then he began again his work of collecting.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: Several years afterwards an epidemic spread over the country.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: Tetsugen gave away what he had collected, to help his people.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: For a third time he started his work, and after twenty years his wish was fulfilled.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 56

page_content: The printing blocks which produced the first edition of sutras can be seen today in the Obaku monastery in Kyoto.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. Publishing the Sutras, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 56

page_content: The Japanese tell their children that Tetsugen made three sets of sutras, and that the first two invisible sets surpass even the last.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: Gisho was ordained as a nun when she was ten years old.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 57

page_content: She received training just as the little boys did.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 57

page_content: When she reached the age of sixteen she traveled from one Zen master to another, studying with them all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: She remained three years with Unzan, six years with Gukei, but was unable to obtain a clear vision.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 57

page_content: At last she went to me master Inzan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: Inzan showed her no distinction at all on account of her sex.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 57

page_content: He scolded her like a thunderstorm.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 57

page_content: He cuffed her to awaken her inner nature.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: Gisho remained with Inzan thirteen years and then she found that which she was seeking!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: In her honor, Inzan wrote a poem:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: This nun studied thirteen years under my guidance.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: In the evening she considered the deepest koans,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: In the morning she was wrapped in other koans.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: The Chinese nun Tetsuma surpassed all before her,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: And since Mujaku none has been so genuine as this Gisho!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: Yet there are many more gates for her to pass through.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: She should receive still more blows from my iron fist.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. Gisho’s Work, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 57

page_content: After Gisho was enlightened she went to the province of Banshu, started her own Zen temple and taught two hundred other nuns until she passed away one year in the month of August.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 58

page_content: The master Soya Shaku passed from this world when he was sixty-one years of age.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 58

page_content: Fulfilling his life’s work, he left a great teaching, far richer than that of most Zen masters.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 58

page_content: His pupils used to sleep in the daytime during midsummer, and while he overlooked this he himself never wasted a minute.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 58

page_content: When he was but twelve year old he was already studying Tendai philosophical speculation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 58

page_content: One summer day the air had been so sultry that Little Soyen stretched his legs and went to sleep while his teacher was away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 58

page_content: Three hours passed when, suddenly waking he hard his master enter, but it was too late.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 58

page_content: There he lay, sprawled across the doorway.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 58

page_content: ‘I beg your pardon, I beg your pardon,’ his teacher whispered, stepping carefully over Soyen’s body as if it were that of some distinguished guest.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Sleeping in the Dayime, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 58

page_content: After this, Soyen never slept again in the afternoon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 59

page_content: ‘Our schoolmaster used to take a nap every afternoon,’ related a disciple of Soyen Shaku.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 59

page_content: ‘We children asked him why he did it and he told us: ‘I go to dreamland to meet the old sages just as Confucius did.’ When Confucius slept, he would dream of ancient sages and later tell his followers about them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 59

page_content: ‘It was extremely hot one day so some of us took a nap.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 59

page_content: Our schoolmaster scolded us.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 59

page_content: “We went to dreamland to meet the ancient sages the same as Confucius did,” we explained.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 59

page_content: “What was the message from those sages?” our schoolmaster demanded.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. In Dreamland, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 59

page_content: One of us replied: “We went to dreamland and met the sages and asked than if our schoolmaster came there every afternoon, but they said they had never seen any such fellow.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Joshu’s Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: Joshu began the study of Zen when he was sixty years old and continued until he was eighty, when he realized Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Joshu’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: He taught from the age of eighty until he was one hundred and twenty.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Joshu’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 60

page_content: A student once asked him: ‘If I haven’t anything in my mind, what shall I do?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Joshu’s Zen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: Joshu replied: ‘Throw it out.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Joshu’s Zen, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: ‘But if I haven’t anything how on I throw it out?’ continued the questioner.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Joshu’s Zen, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 60

page_content: ‘Well,’ said Joshu, ‘then carry it out.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 61

page_content: When Mamiya, who later became a well-known preacher, went to a teacher for personal guidance, he was asked to explain the sound of one hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 61

page_content: Mamiya concentrated upon what the sound of one hand might be.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 61

page_content: ‘You are not working hard enough,’ his teacher told him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 61

page_content: ‘You are too attached to food, wealth, things, and that sound.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 61

page_content: It would he better if you died.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 61

page_content: That would solve the problem.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 61

page_content: The next time Mamiya appeared before his teacher he was again asked what he had to show regarding the sound of one hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 61

page_content: Mamiya at once fell over as if he were dead.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 61

page_content: ‘You are dead all right’ observed the teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 61

page_content: ‘But how about that sound?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 61

page_content: ‘I haven’t solved that yet,’ replied Mamiya, looking up.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Dead Man’s Answer, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 61

page_content: ‘Dead men do not speak,’ said the teacher.’ Get out!’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: Tosui was a well-known Zen teacher of his time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: He had lived in several temples and taught in various provinces.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: The last temple he visited accumulated so many adherents that Tosui told them he was going to quit the lecture business entirely.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: He advised them to disperse and to go wherever they desired.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 62

page_content: After that no one could find any trace of him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: Three years later one of his disciple discovered him living with some beggars under a bridge in Kyoto.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: He at once implored Tosui to teach him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: ‘If you can do as I do for even a couple of days, I might,’ Tosui replied.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: So the former disciple dressed as a beggar and spent a day with Tosui.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: The following day one of the beggars died.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 62

page_content: Tosui and his pupil carried the body off at midnight and buried it on a mountainside.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 62

page_content: After that they returned to their shelter under the bridge.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: Tosui slept soundly the remainder of the night, but the disciple could not sleep.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: When morning came Tosui said: ‘We do not have to beg food today.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 62

page_content: Our dead friend has left some over there.’ But the disciple was unable to eat a single bite of it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 62

page_content: ‘I have said you could not do as I.’ concluded Tosui.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Zen in a Beggar’s Life, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 62

page_content: ‘Get out of here and do not bother me again.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. The Thief Who Became a Disciple, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 63

page_content: One evening as Shichiri Kojun was reciting sutras a thief with a sharp sword entered, demanding either his money or his life, Shichiri told him: ‘Do not disturb me.
