page_content: In haste the cook went to the garden with his curved knife and cut off the tops of green vegetables, chopped them together, and made soup, unaware that in his haste he had included a part of a snake in the vegetables.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 69. Eating the Blame, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 91

page_content: The followers of Fugai thought they never had tasted such good soup.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 69. Eating the Blame, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 91

page_content: But when the master himself found the snake’s head in his bowl, he summoned the cook, ‘What is this?’ he demanded, holding up the head of the snake.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 69. Eating the Blame, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 91

page_content: ‘Oh, thank you, master,’ replied the cook, taking the morsel and eating it quickly.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 70. The Most Valuable Thing in the World, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: Sozan, a Chinese Zen master, was asked by a student: ‘What is the most valuable thing in the world?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 70. The Most Valuable Thing in the World, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: The master replied: The head of a dead cat.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 70. The Most Valuable Thing in the World, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: ‘Why is the head of a dead cat the most valuable thing in the world?’ inquired the student.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 70. The Most Valuable Thing in the World, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 92

page_content: Sozan replied: ‘Because no one can name its price.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: The pupils of the Tendai School used to study meditation before Zen entered Japan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: Four of them who were intimate friends promised one another to observe seven days of silence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: On the first day all were silent Their meditation had begun auspiciously, but when night came and the oil-lamps were growing dim one of the pupils could not help exclaiming to a servant: ‘Fix those lamps.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: The second pupil was surprised to hear the first one talk.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: ‘We are not supposed to say a word,’ he remarked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: ‘You two are stupid.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 93

page_content: Why did you talk?’ asked the third.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 71. Learning to be Silent, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 93

page_content: ‘I am the only one who has not talked,’ muttered the fourth pupil.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 72. The Blockhead Lord, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 94

page_content: Two Zen teachers Daigu and Gudo, were invited to visit a lord.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 72. The Blockhead Lord, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 94

page_content: Upon arriving, Gudo said to the lord: ‘You are wise by nature and have an inborn ability to learn Zen.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 72. The Blockhead Lord, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 94

page_content: ‘Nonsense,’ said Daigu ‘Why do you flatter this blockheads?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 72. The Blockhead Lord, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 94

page_content: He may be a lord, but he doesn’t know anything of Zen.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 72. The Blockhead Lord, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 94

page_content: So, instead of building a temple for Gudo the lord built it for Daigu and studied Zen with him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 95

page_content: Zen pupils take a vow that even if they are killed by their teacher, they intend to learn Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 95

page_content: Usually they cut a finger and seal their resolution with blood.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 95

page_content: In time the vow has become a men formality, and for this reason the pupil who died by the hand of Ekido was made to appear a martyr.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 95

page_content: Ekido had become a severe teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 95

page_content: His pupils feared him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 95

page_content: One of them on duty, striking the gong to tell the time of day, missed his beats when his eye was attracted by a beautiful girl passing the temple gate.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 95

page_content: At that moment Ekido, who was directly behind him, hit him with a stick and the shock happened to kill him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 95

page_content: The pupil’s guardian, hearing of the accident went directly to Ekido.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 95

page_content: Knowing that he was not to blame he praised the master for his severe teaching.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 95

page_content: Ekido’s attitude was just the same as if the pupil were still alive.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 73. Ten Successors, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 95

page_content: After this took place, he was able to produce under his guidance more than ten enlightened successors, a very unusual number.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 96

page_content: Ryokan devoted his life to the study of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 96

page_content: One day he heard that his nephew, despite the admonitions of relatives, was spending his money on a courtesan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 96

page_content: Inasmuch as the nephew had taken Ryokan’s place in managing the family estate and the property was in danger of being dissipated, the relatives asked Ryokan to do something about it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 96

page_content: Ryokan had to travel a long way to visit his nephew, whom he had not seen for many years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 96

page_content: The nephew seemed pleased to meet his uncle again and invited him to remain overnight.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 96

page_content: All night Ryokan sat in meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 96

page_content: As he was departing in the morning he said to the young man: ‘I must be getting old, my hand shakes so.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 96

page_content: Will you help me tie the string of my straw sandal?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 96

page_content: The nephew helped him willingly.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 96

page_content: ‘Thank you,’ finished Ryokan, ‘you see, a man becomes older and feebler day by day.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 96

page_content: Take good care of yourself.’ Then Ryokan left, never mentioning a word about the courtesan or the complaints of the relatives.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 74. True Reformation, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 96

page_content: But, from that morning on, the dissipations of the nephew ended.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: A Zen student came to Bankei and complained: ‘Master, I have an ungovernable temper.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 97

page_content: How can I cure it?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: ‘You have something very strange,’ replied Bankei.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 97

page_content: ‘Let me see what you have.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: ‘Just now I cannot show it to you,’ replied the other.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: ‘When can you show it to me?’ asked Bankei.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: ‘It arises unexpectedly,’ replied the student.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 97

page_content: ‘Then,’ concluded Bankei, ‘it must not be your own nature.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 97

page_content: If it were, you could show it to me at any time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 97

page_content: When you were born you did not have if and your parents did not give it to you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 75. Temper, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 97

page_content: Think that over.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 98

page_content: Hogen, a Chinese Zen teacher, lived alone in a small temple in the country.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 98

page_content: One day four traveling monks appeared and asked if they might make a fire in his yard to warm themselves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 98

page_content: While they were building the fire, Hogen heard them arguing about subjectivity and objectivity.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 98

page_content: He joined them and said: There is a big stone.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 98

page_content: Do you consider it to be inside or outside your mind?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 98

page_content: One of the monks replied: ‘From the Buddhist viewpoint everything is an objectification of mind, so I would say that the stone is inside my mind.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 98

page_content: ‘Your head must feel very heavy’, observed Hogen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 76. The Stone Mind, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 98

page_content: ‘if you are carrying around a stone like that in your mind.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Zengetsu, a Chinese master of the T’ang dynasty, wrote the following advice for his pupils:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Living in the world yet not forming attachments to the dust of the world is the way of a true Zen student.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: When witnessing the good action of another encourage yourself to follow his example.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Hearing of the mistaken action of another, advise yourself not to emulate it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Even though alone in a dark room, be as if you were facing a noble guest.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Express your feelings, but become no more expressive than your true nature.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Poverty is your treasure.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Never exchange it for an easy life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: A person may appear a fool and yet not be one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: He may only be guarding his wisdom carefully.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Virtues are the fruit of self-discipline and do not drop from heaven of themselves as does rain or snow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Modesty is the foundation of all virtues.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Let your neighbors discover you before you make yourself known to them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: A noble heart never forces itself forward.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Its words are a rare gems seldom displayed and of great value.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: To a sincere student, every day is a fortunate day.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Time passes but he never lags behind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 10, sentence number 3 on page 99

page_content: Neither glory nor shame can move him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Censure yourself, never another.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Do not discuss right and wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Some things though right, were considered wrong for generations.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Since the value of righteousness may be recognized after centuries, there is no need to crave an immediate appreciation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 99

page_content: Live with cause and leave results to the great law of the universe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 77. No Attachment to Dust, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 99

page_content: Pass each day in peaceful contemplation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 100

page_content: A rich man asked Sengai to write something for the continued prosperity of his family so that it might be treasured from generation to generation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 100

page_content: Sengai obtained a large sheet of paper and wrote: ‘Father dies, son dies, grandson dies.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 100

page_content: The rich man became angry.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 100

page_content: I asked you to write something for the happiness of my family!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 100

page_content: Why do you make such a joke as this?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 100

page_content: ‘No joke is intended,’ explained Sengai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 100

page_content: ‘If before you yourself die your son should die, this would grieve you greatly.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 100

page_content: If your grandson should pass away before your son, both of you would be broken hearted.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 100

page_content: If your family, generation after generation, passes away in the order I have named, it will be the natural course of life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 78. Real Prosperity, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 100

page_content: I call this real prosperity.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: A woman of Nagasaki named Kame was one of the few makers of incense burners in Japan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: Such a burner is a work of art to be used only in a tea room or before a family shrine.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: Kame whose father before her had been such an artist, was fond of drinking.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: She also smoked and associated with men most of the time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 101

page_content: Whatever she made a little money she gave a feast inviting artists, poets, carpenters, workers, men of many vocations and avocations.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 101

page_content: In their association she evolved her designs.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: Kame was exceedingly slow in creating, but when her work was finished it was always a masterpiece.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: Her burners were treasured in homes whose womenfolk never drank, smoked, or associated freely with men.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: The mayor of Nagasaki once requested Kame to design an incense burner for him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: She delayed doing so until almost half a year had passed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 101

page_content: At that time the mayor, who had been promoted to office in a distant city, visited her.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 101

page_content: He urged Kame to begin work on his burner.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: At last receiving the inspiration, Kame made the incense burner.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: After it was completed she placed it upon a table.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 101

page_content: She looked at it long and carefully.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 101

page_content: She smoked and drank before it as if it were her own company.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 101

page_content: All day she observed it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 101

page_content: At last, picking up a hammer, Kame smashed it to bits.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 79. Incense Burner, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 101

page_content: She saw it was not the perfect creation her mind demanded.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 80.The Real Miracle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 102

page_content: When Bankei was preaching at Ryumon temple, a Shinshu priest, who believed in salvation through the repetition of the name of the Buddha of Love, was jealous of his large audience and wanted to debate with him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 80.The Real Miracle, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 102

page_content: Bankei was in the midst of a talk when the priest appeared but the fellow made such a disturbance that Bankei stopped his discourse and asked about the noise.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 80.The Real Miracle, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 102

page_content: ‘The founder of our sect,’ boasted the priest, ‘had such miraculous powers that he held a brush in his hand on one bank of the river, his attendant held up a paper on the other bank, and the teacher wrote the holy name of Amida through the air.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 80.The Real Miracle, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 102

page_content: Can you do such a wonderful thing?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 80.The Real Miracle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 102

page_content: Bankei replied lightly: ‘Perhaps your fox can perform that trick, but that is not the manner of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 80.The Real Miracle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 102

page_content: My miracle is that when I feel hungry I eat, and when I feel thirsty I drink.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: Gasan was sitting at the bedside of Tekisui three days before his teacher’s passing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 103

page_content: Tekisui had already chosen him as his successor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: A temple recently had burned and Gasan was busy rebuilding the structure.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 103

page_content: Tekisui asked him: ‘What are you going to do when you get the temple rebuilt?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: ‘When your sickness is over we want you to speak there,’ said Gasan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: ‘Suppose I do not live until then?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: ‘Then we will get someone else,’ replied Gasan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: Suppose you cannot find anyone?’ continued Tekisui.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 103

page_content: Gasan answered loudly: ‘Don’t ask such foolish questions.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 81. Just Go to Sleep, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 103

page_content: Just go to sleep.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: Yamaoka Tesshu, as a young student of Zen, visited one master after another.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 104

page_content: He called upon Dokuon of Shokoku.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: Desiring to show his attainment, he said: The mind, Buddha, and sentient beings, after all, do not exist.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 104

page_content: The true nature of phenomena is emptiness.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 104

page_content: There is no realization, no delusion, no sage, no mediocrity.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 104

page_content: There is no giving and nothing to be received.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: Dokuon, who was smoking quietly, said nothing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 104

page_content: Suddenly he whacked Yamaoka with his bamboo pipe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 104

page_content: This made the youth quite angry.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 82. Nothing Exists, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 104

page_content: ‘If nothing exists,’ inquired Dokuon, ‘where did this anger come from?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 105

page_content: Hyakujo, the Chinese Zen master, used to labor with his pupils even at the age of eighty, trimming the gardens, cleaning the grounds, and pruning the trees.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 105

page_content: The pupils felt sorry to see the old teacher working so hard, but they knew he would not listen to their advice to stop, so they hid away his tools.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 105

page_content: That day the master did not eat.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 105

page_content: The next day he did not eat, nor the next.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 105

page_content: ‘He may be angry because we have hidden his tools,’ the pupils surmised.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 105

page_content: ‘We had better put than back.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 105

page_content: The day they did, the teacher worked and ate the same as before.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 83. No Work, No Food, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 105

page_content: In the evening he instructed them: ‘No work no food.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 84. True Friends, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: A long time ago in China there were two friends, one who played the harp skillfully and one who listened skillfully.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 84. True Friends, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: When the one played or sang about a mountain, the other would say: ‘I can see the mountain before us.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 84. True Friends, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: When the one played about water, the listener would exclaim: ‘Here is the running stream!’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 84. True Friends, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 106

page_content: But the listener fell sick and died.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 84. True Friends, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 106

page_content: The first friend cut the strings of his harp and never played again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 84. True Friends, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 106

page_content: Since that time the cutting of harp strings has always been a sign of intimate friendship.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: Ikkyu, the Zen master, was very clever even as a boy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 107

page_content: His teacher had a precious teacup, a rare antique.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 107

page_content: Ikkyu happened to break this cup and was greatly perplexed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 107

page_content: Hearing the footsteps of his teacher, he held the pieces of the cup behind him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 107

page_content: When the master appeared, Ikkyu asked: ‘Why do people have to die?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: ‘This is natural,’ explained the older man.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 107

page_content: ‘Everything has to die and has just so long to live.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 85. Time to Die, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: Ikkyu, producing the shattered cup, added: ‘It was time for your cup to die.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: Zen masters give personal guidance in a secluded room.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 107

page_content: No one enters while teacher and pupil are together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: Mokurai, the Zen master of Kennin temple in Kyoto, used to enjoy talking with merchants and newspapermen as well as with his pupils.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 107

page_content: A certain tubmaker was almost illiterate.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 107

page_content: He would ask foolish questions of Mokurai have tea, and then go away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: One day while the tubmaker was there Mokurai wished to give personal guidance to a disciple so he asked the tubmaker to wait in another room.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: ‘I understand you are a living Buddha,’ the man protested.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 107

page_content: ‘Even the stone Buddha’s in the temple never refuse the numerous persons who come together before them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 107

page_content: Why then should I be excluded?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 86. The Living Buddha and the Tubmaker, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 107

page_content: Mokurai had to go outside to see his disciple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 108

page_content: A Zen master named Gettan lived in the latter part of the Tokugawa era.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 108

page_content: He used to say: ‘ There are three kinds of disciples: those who impart Zen to others, those who maintain the temples and shrines, and then there are the rice bags and the clothes-hangers.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 108

page_content: Gasan expressed the same idea.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 108

page_content: When he was studying under Tekisui, his teacher was very severe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 108

page_content: Sometimes he even beat him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 108

page_content: Other pupils would not stand this kind of teaching and quit.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 108

page_content: Gasan remained saying: ‘A poor disciple utilizes a teacher’s influence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 108

page_content: A fair disciple admires a teacher’s kindness.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 87. Three Kinds of Disciples, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 108

page_content: A good disciple grows strong under a teacher’s discipline.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: A well-known Japanese poet was asked how to compose a Chinese poem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 109

page_content: The usual Chinese poem is four lines,’ he explained.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 109

page_content: The first line contains the initial phrase; the second line, the continuation of that phase; the third line turns from this subject and begins a new one; and the fourth line brings the first three lines together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 109

page_content: A popular Japanese song illustrates this:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: ‘Two daughters of a silk merchant live in Kyoto,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: The elder is twenty, the younger, eighteen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: A soldier may kill with his sword,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 88. How to Write a Chinese Poem, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 109

page_content: But these girls slay men with their eyes.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: Zen teachers train their young pupils to express themselves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 110

page_content: Two Zen temples; each had a child protégé.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 110

page_content: One child going to obtain vegetables each morning would meet the other on the way.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘Where are you going?’ asked the one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘I am going wherever my feet go.’ the other responded.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: This reply puzzled the first child who went to his teacher for help.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘Tomorrow morning,’ the teacher told him, ‘when you meet that little fellow, ask him the same question.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 110

page_content: He will give you the same answer, and then you ask him: “Suppose have no feet, then where are you going!” That will fix him.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: The children met again the following morning.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 110

page_content: ‘Where are you going?’ asked the first child.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘I am going wherever the wind blows,’ answered the other.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: This again nonplussed the youngster, who took his defeat to his teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘Ask him where he is going if there is no wind,’ suggested the teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: The next day the children met a third time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘Where are you going?’ asked the first child.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 89. Zen Dialogue, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 110

page_content: ‘I am going to market to buy vegetables,’ the other replied.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: Tangen had studied with Sengai since childhood.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: When he was twenty he wanted to have his teacher and visit others for comparative study, but Sengai would not permit this.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 111

page_content: Every time Tangen suggested it, Sengai would give him a rap on the head.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: Finally Tangen asked an elder brother to coax permission from Sengai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: This the brother did and then reported to Tangen:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: ‘It is arranged.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: I have fixed it for you to start on your pilgrimage at once.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: Tangen went to Sengai to thank him for his permission.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: The master answered by giving him another rap.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: When Tangen related this to his elder brother the other said: ‘What is the matter?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: Sengai has no business giving permission and then changing his mind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 111

page_content: I will tell him so.’ And off he went to see the teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 111

page_content: ‘I did not cancel my permission,’ said Sengai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 90. The Last Rap, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 111

page_content: ‘I just wished to give him one last smack over the head, for when he returns he will be enlightened and I will not be able to reprimand him again.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: Matajuro Yagyu was the son of a famous swordsman.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: His father, believing that his son’s work was too mediocre to anticipate mastership, disowned him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: So Matajuro went to Mount Fuhra and there found the famous swordsman Banzo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: But Banzo confirmed the father’s judgment.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 112

page_content: ‘You wish to learn swordsmanship under my guidance?’ asked Banzo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 112

page_content: ‘You cannot fulfill the requirements.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘But if I work hard, how many years will it take me to be come a master?’ persisted the youth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘The rest of your life,’ replied Banzo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘I cannot wait that long,’ explained Matajuro.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: ‘I am willing to pass through any hardship if only you will teach me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 112

page_content: If I become your devoted servant, how long might it be?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘Oh, maybe ten years,’ Banzo relented.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘My father is getting old, and soon I must take care of him,’ continued Matajuro.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: ‘If I work far more intensively, how long would it take me!’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘Oh, maybe thirty years.’ said Banzo.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘Why is that?’ asked Matajuro.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: ‘First you say ten and now thirty years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 112

page_content: I will undergo any hardship to master this art in the shortest time!’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘Well,’ said Banzo, ‘in that case you will have to remain with me for seventy years.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: A man in such a hurry as you are to get results seldom learns quickly.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: ‘Very well.’ declared the youth, understanding at last that he was being rebuked for impatience, ‘I agree.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: Matajuro was told never to speak of fencing and never to touch a sword.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: He cooked for his master, washed the dishes, made his bed, and cleaned the yard, cared for the garden, all without a word of swordsmanship.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: Three years passed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 112

page_content: Still Matajuro labored on.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 13, sentence number 3 on page 112

page_content: Thinking of his future he was sad.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 13, sentence number 4 on page 112

page_content: He had not even begun to learn the art to which he had devoted his life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 112

page_content: But one day Banzo crept up behind him and gave him a terrific blow with a wooden sword.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 15, sentence number 1 on page 113

page_content: The following day, when Matajuro was cooking rice, Banzo, again sprang upon him unexpectedly.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 16, sentence number 1 on page 113

page_content: After that, day and night, Matajuro had to defend himself from unexpected thrusts.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 16, sentence number 2 on page 113

page_content: Not a moment passed in any day that he did not have to think of the taste of Banzo’s sword.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 17, sentence number 1 on page 113

page_content: He learned so rapidly he brought smiles to the face of his master.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 91. The Taste of Banzo’s Sword, paragraph number 17, sentence number 2 on page 113

page_content: Matajuro became the greatest swordsman in the land.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 114

page_content: Hakuin used to tell his pupils about an old woman who had a teashop, praising her understanding of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 114

page_content: The pupils refused to believe what he told them and would go to the teashop to find out for themselves.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 114

page_content: Whenever the woman saw them coming she could tell it once whether they had come for tea or to look into her grasp of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 114

page_content: In the former case, she would serve them graciously.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 114

page_content: In the latter, she would beckon to the pupils to come behind her screen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 114

page_content: The instant they obeyed, she would strike than with a fire-poker.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 92. Fire-Poker Zen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 114

page_content: Nine out of ten of them could not escape her beating.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: Encho was a famous storyteller.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: His tales of love stirred the hearts of his listeners.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: When he narrated a story of war, it was as if the listeners themselves were on the field of battle.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: One day Encho met Yamaoka Tesshu, a layman who had almost embraced master hood in Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: ‘I understand,’ said Yamaoka, ‘you are the best storyteller in our land and that you make people cry or laugh at will.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: Tell me my favorite story of the Peach Boy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 115

page_content: When I was a little tot I used to sleep beside my mother, and she often related this legend.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 115

page_content: In the middle of the story I would fall asleep.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 115

page_content: Tell it to me just is my mother did.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: Encho dared not attempt to do this.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: He requested time to study.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: Several months later he went to Yamaoka and said,

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: ‘Please give me the opportunity to tell you the story.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: ‘Some other day,’ answered Yamaoka.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: Encho was keenly disappointed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: He studied further and tried again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 115

page_content: Yamaoka rejected him many times.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 115

page_content: When Encho would start to talk Yamaoka would stop him, saying: ‘You are not yet like my mother.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 115

page_content: It took Encho five years to be able to tell Yamaoka the legend as his mother had told it to him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 93. Storyteller’s Zen, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 115

page_content: In this way, Yamaoka imparted Zen to Encho.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: Many pupils were studying meditation under the Zen master Sengai.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 116

page_content: One of them used to arise at night, climb over the temple wall, and go to town on a pleasure jaunt.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: Sengai, inspecting the dormitory quarters, found this pupil missing one night and also discovered the high stool he had used to scale the wall.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 116

page_content: Sengai removed the stool and stood there in its place.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: When the wanderer returned, not knowing that Sengai was the stool he put his feet on the master’s head and jumped down into the grounds.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 116

page_content: Discovering what he had done, he was aghast.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: Sengai said: ‘It is very chilly in the early morning.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 116

page_content: Do be careful not to catch cold yourself.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 94. Midnight Excursion, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 116

page_content: The pupil never went out at night again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: Bassui wrote the following letter to one of his disciples who was about to die:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: The essence of your mind is not born so it will never die.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 117

page_content: It is not an existence, which is perishable.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 117

page_content: It is not an emptiness, which is a mere void.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 117

page_content: It has neither color nor form.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 117

page_content: It enjoys no pleasures and suffers no pain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 117

page_content: ‘I know you are very ill. Like a good Zen student, you are facing that sickness squarely.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 117

page_content: You may not know exactly who is suffering, but question yourself: What is the essence of this mind?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 117

page_content: Think only of this.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 117

page_content: You will need no more.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 117

page_content: Covet nothing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 95. A Letter to a Dying Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 117

page_content: Your end which is endless is as a snowflake dissolving in the pure air.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: A Zen master named Gisan asked a young student to bring him a pail of water to cool his bath.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: The student brought the water and, after cooling the bath, threw on to the ground the little that was left over.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: ‘You dunce!’ the master scolded him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 118

page_content: ‘Why didn’t you give the rest of the water to the plants?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 118

page_content: What right have you to waste even a drop of water in this temple?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 118

page_content: The young student attained Zen in that instant.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 96.A Drop of Water, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 118

page_content: He changed his name to Tekisui, which means a drop of water.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 119

page_content: In early times in Japan, bamboo-and-paper lanterns wee used with candles inside.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 119

page_content: A blind man, visiting a friend one night was offered a lantern to carry home with him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 119

page_content: ‘I do not need a lantern,’ he said.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 119

page_content: Darkness or light is all the same to me’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 119

page_content: ‘I know you do not need a lantern to find your way,’ his friend replied, ‘but if you don’t have one someone else may run into you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 119

page_content: So you must take it.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 119

page_content: The blind man started off with the lantern and before he had walked very far someone ran squarely into him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 119

page_content: ‘Look out where you are going!’ he exclaimed to the stranger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 119

page_content: ‘Can’t you see this lantern?’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 97. Teaching the Ultimate, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 119

page_content: ‘Your candle has burned out brother,’ replied the stranger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: Kitano Gempo, abbot of Eihei temple was ninety-two years old when he passed away in the year 1933.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: He endeavored his whole life not to be attached to anything.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 120

page_content: As a wandering mendicant when he was twenty he happened to meet a traveler who smoked tobacco.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 120

page_content: As they walked together down a mountain road they stopped under a tree to rest.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 120

page_content: The traveler offered Kitano a smoke, which he accepted, as he was very hungry at the time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: ‘How pleasant this smoking is,’ he commented.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: The other gave him an extra pipe and tobacco and they parted.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: Kitano felt: ‘Such pleasant things may disturb meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: Before this goes too far, I will stop now.’ So he threw the smoking outfit away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: When he was twenty-three years old he studied I-King, the profoundest doctrine of the universe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: It was winter at the time and he needed some heavy clothes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 120

page_content: He wrote to his teacher, who lived a hundred miles away, telling him of his need, and gave the letter to a traveler to deliver.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 120

page_content: Almost the whole winter passed and neither answer nor clothes arrived.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 120

page_content: So Kitano resorted to the prescience of I-King, which also teaches the art of divination, to determine whether or not his letter had miscarried.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 120

page_content: He found that this had been the case.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 120

page_content: A letter afterwards from his teacher made no mention of clothes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: ‘If I perform such accurate determinative work with I-King, I may neglect my meditation,’ felt Kitano.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: So he gave up this marvelous teaching and never resorted to its powers again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 120

page_content: When he was twenty-eight he studied Chinese calligraphy and poetry.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 120

page_content: He grew so skilful in these arts that his teacher praised him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 98. Non-Attachment, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 120

page_content: Kitano mused: ‘If I don’t stop now, I’ll be a poet not a Zen teacher.’ So he never wrote another poem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 121

page_content: Tosui was the Zen master who left the formalism of temples to live under a bridge with beggars.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 121

page_content: When he was getting very old, a friend helped him to earn his living without begging.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 121

page_content: He showed Tosui how to collect rice and manufacture vinegar from it and Tosui did this until he passed away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 121

page_content: While Tosui was making vinegar, one of the beggars gave him a picture of the Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 121

page_content: Tosui hung it on the wall of his hut and put a sign beside it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 121

page_content: The sign read:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 121

page_content: ‘Mr.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 121

page_content: Amida Buddha: This little loom is quite narrow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 121

page_content: I can let you remain as a transient.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 99. Tasui’s Vinegar, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 121

page_content: But don’t think I am asking you to help me to be reborn in your paradise.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 122

page_content: Shochi was a one-eyed teacher of Zen, sparkling with enlightenment.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 122

page_content: He taught his disciple in Tofuku temple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 122

page_content: Day and night the whole temple stood in silence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 122

page_content: There was no sound at all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 122

page_content: Even the reciting of sutras was abolished by the teacher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 122

page_content: His pupils had nothing to do but meditate.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 122
