page_content: What the master passed away, an old neighbor heard the ringing of bells and the recitation of sutras.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 100. The Silent Temple, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 122

page_content: Then she knew Shoichi had gone.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 123

page_content: Buddha said:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 123

page_content: ‘I consider the positions of kings and rulers as that of dust motes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 123

page_content: I observe treasure of gold and gems as so many bricks and pebbles.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 123

page_content: I look upon the finest silken robes as tattered rags.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 123

page_content: I see myriad worlds of the universe as small seeds of fruit, and the greatest lake in India as a drop of oil on my foot.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 123

page_content: I perceive the teachings of the world to be the illusion of, magicians.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 123

page_content: I discern the highest conception of emancipation as golden brocade in a dream, and view the holy path of the illuminated one as flowers appearing in one’s eyes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 7 on page 123

page_content: I see meditation as a pillar of a mountain, Nirvana as a nightmare of daytime.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 101. Buddha’s Zen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 8 on page 123

page_content: I look upon the judgment of right and wrong as the serpentine dance of a dragon, and the rise and fall of beliefs as but traces left by the four seasons.’

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: THE GATELESS GATE, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 125

page_content: by Ekai, called Mumon

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: THE GATELESS GATE, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 126

page_content: Transcribed by Nyogen Senzaki and Paul Reps

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: A monk asked Joshu, a Chinese Zen master: “Has a dog Buddha-nature or not?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: Joshu answered: “Mu.” *

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: Mumon’s comment: To realize Zen one has to pass through the barrier of the patriarchs.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: Enlightenment always comes after the road of thinking is blocked.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 127

page_content: If you do not pass the barrier of the patriarchs or if your thinking road is not blocked, whatever you think, whatever you do, is like a tangling ghost.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 127

page_content: You may ask: What is a barrier of a patriarch?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 127

page_content: This one word, Mu, is it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: This is the barrier of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: If you pass through it you will see Joshu face to face.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 127

page_content: Then you can work hand in hand with the whole line of patriarchs.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 127

page_content: Is this not a pleasant thing to do?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: If you want to pass this barrier, you must work through every bone in your body, through every pore of your skin, filled with this question: What is Mu?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: and carry it day and night.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 127

page_content: Do not believe it is the common negative symbol meaning nothing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 127

page_content: It is not nothingness, the opposite of existence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 5, sentence number 5 on page 127

page_content: If you really want to pass this barrier, you should feel like drinking a hot iron ball that you can neither swallow nor spit out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: Then your previous lesser knowledge disappears.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: As a fruit ripening in season, your subjectivity and objectivity naturally become one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 127

page_content: It is like a dumb man who has had a dream.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 127

page_content: He knows about it but he cannot tell it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: When he enters this condition his ego-shell is crushed and he can shake the heaven and move the earth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: He is like a great warrior with a sharp sword.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 127

page_content: If a Buddha stands in his way, he will cut him down; if a patriarch offers him any obstacle, he will kill him; and he will be free in his way of birth and death.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 127

page_content: He can enter any world as if it were his own playground.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 127

page_content: I will tell you how to do this with this koan:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 127

page_content: Just concentrate your whole energy into this Mu, and do not allow any discontinuation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 127

page_content: When you enter this Mu and there is no discontinuation, your attainment will be as a candle burning and illuminating the whole universe.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 128

page_content: Has a dog Buddha-nature?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 128

page_content: This is the most serious question of all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. Joshu’s Dog, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 128

page_content: If you say yes or no, You lose your own Buddha-nature.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: Once when Hyakujo delivered some Zen lectures an old man attended them, unseen by the monks.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: At the end of each talk when the monks left so did he.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 129

page_content: But one day he remained after they had gone, and Hyakujo asked him: “Who are you?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: The old man replied: “I am not a human being, but I was a human being when the Kashapa Buddha preached in this world.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: I was a Zen master and lived on this mountain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 129

page_content: At that time one of my students asked me whether or not the enlightened man is subject to the law of causation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 129

page_content: I answered him: ‘The enlightened man is not subject to the law of causation.’ For this answer evidencing a clinging to absoluteness I became a fox for five hundred rebirths, and I am still a fox.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 129

page_content: Will you save me from this condition with your Zen words and let me get out of a fox’s body?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 129

page_content: Now may I ask you: Is the enlightened man subject to the law of causation?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: Hyakujo said: “The enlightened man is one with the law of causation.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: At the words of Hyakujo the old man was enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: “I am emancipated,” he said, paying homage with a deep bow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 129

page_content: “I am no more a fox, but I have to leave my body in my dwelling place behind this mountain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 129

page_content: Please perform my funeral as a monk.” Then he disappeared.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: The next day Hyakujo gave an order through the chief monk to prepare to attend the funeral of a monk.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: “No one was sick in the infirmary,” wondered the monks.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 129

page_content: “What does our teacher mean?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: After dinner Hyakujo led the monks out and around the mountain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: In a cave, with his staff he poked out the corpse of an old fox and then performed the ceremony of cremation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: That evening Hyakujo gave a talk to the monks and told them this story about the law of causation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: Obaku, upon hearing the story, asked Hyakujo: “I understand that a long time ago because a certain person gave a wrong Zen answer he became a fox for five hundred rebirths.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 129

page_content: Now I want to ask: If some modern master is asked many questions and he always gives the right answer, what will become of him?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 129

page_content: Hyakujo said: “You come here near me and I will tell you.” Obaku went near Hyakujo and slapped the teacher’s face with his hand, for he knew this was the answer his teacher intended to give him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: Hyakujo clapped his hands and laughed at this discernment.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 130

page_content: “I thought a Persian had a red beard,” he said, “and now I know a Persian who has a red beard.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: Mumon’s comment: “The enlightened man is not subject.” How can this answer make the monk a fox?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: “The enlightened man is one with the law of causation.” How can this answer make the fox emancipated?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: To understand this clearly one has to have just one eye.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 130

page_content: Controlled or not controlled?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 130

page_content: The same dice shows two faces.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Hyakujo’s Fox, paragraph number 14, sentence number 3 on page 130

page_content: Not controlled or controlled, Both are a grievous error.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: Gutei raised his finger whenever he was asked a question about Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: A boy attendant began to imitate him in this way.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 131

page_content: When anyone asked the boy what his master had preached about, the boy would raise his finger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: Gutei heard about the boy’s mischief.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: He seized him and cut off his finger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 131

page_content: The boy cried and ran away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 131

page_content: Gutei called and stopped him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 131

page_content: When the boy turned his head to Gutei, Gutei raised up his own finger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 2, sentence number 6 on page 131

page_content: In that instant the boy was enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: When Gutei was about to pass from this world he gathered his monks around him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: “I attained my finger-Zen,” he said, “from my teacher Tenryu, and in my whole life I could not exhaust it.” Then he passed away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Enlightenment, which Gutei and the boy attained, has nothing to do with a finger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: If anyone clings to a finger, Tenryu will be so disappointed that he will annihilate Gutei, the boy, and the clinger all together.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: Gutei cheapens the teaching of Tenryu, Emancipating the boy with a knife.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 131

page_content: Compared to the Chinese god who pushed aside a mountain with one

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Gutei’s Finger, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 131

page_content: hand Old Gutei is a poor imitator.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 132

page_content: Wakuan complained when he saw a picture of bearded Bodhidharma: “Why hasn’t that fellow a beard?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 132

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If you want to study Zen, you must study it with your heart.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 132

page_content: When you attain realization, it must be true realization.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 132

page_content: You yourself must have the face of the great Bodhidharma to see him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 132

page_content: Just one such glimpse will be enough.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 132

page_content: But if you say you met him, you never saw him at all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 132

page_content: One should not discuss a dream In front of a simpleton.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 132

page_content: Why has Bodhidharma no beard?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. A Beardless Foreigner, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 132

page_content: What an absurd question!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: Kyogen said: “Zen is like a man hanging in a tree by his teeth over a precipice.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: His hands grasp no branch, his feet rest on no limb, and under the tree another person asks him: ‘Why did Bodhidharma come to China from India?’”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: If the man in the tree does not answer, he fails; and if he does answer, he falls and loses his life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: Now what shall he do?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: Mumon’s comment: In such a predicament the most talented eloquence is of no use.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 133

page_content: If you have memorized all the sutras, you cannot use them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 133

page_content: When you can give the right answer, even though your past road was one of death, you open up a new road of life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 133

page_content: But if you cannot answer, you should live ages hence and ask the future Buddha, Maitreya.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Kyogen Mounts the Tree, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 133

page_content: Kyogen is truly a fool Spreading that ego-killing poison That closes his pupils’ mouths And lets their tears stream from their dead eyes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 134

page_content: When Buddha was in Grdhrakuta mountain he turned a flower in his fingers and held it before his listeners.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 134

page_content: Every one was silent.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 134

page_content: Only Maha-Kashapa smiled at this revelation, although he tried to control the lines of his face.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 134

page_content: Buddha said: “I have the eye of the true teaching, the heart of Nirvana, the true aspect of non-form, and the ineffable stride of Dharma.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 134

page_content: It is not expressed by words, but especially transmitted beyond teaching.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 134

page_content: This teaching I have given to Maha-Kashapa.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 134

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Golden-faced Gautama thought he could cheat anyone.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 134

page_content: He made the good listeners as bad, and sold dog meat under the sign of mutton.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 134

page_content: And he himself thought it was wonderful.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 134

page_content: What if all the audience had laughed together?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 134

page_content: How could he have transmitted the teaching?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 6 on page 134

page_content: And again, if Maha-Kashapa had not smiled, how could he have transmitted the teaching?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 3, sentence number 7 on page 134

page_content: If he says that realization can be transmitted, he is like the city slicker that cheats the country dub, and if he says it cannot be transmitted, why does he approve of Maha-Kashapa?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 134

page_content: At the turning of a flower His disguise was exposed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Buddha Twirls a Flower, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 134

page_content: No one in heaven or earth can surpass Maha-Kashapa’s wrinkled face.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: A monk told Joshu: “I have just entered the monastery.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 135

page_content: Please teach me.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: Joshu asked: “Have you eaten your rice porridge?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: The monk replied: “I have eaten.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: Joshu said: “Then you had better wash your bowl.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: At that moment the monk was enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Joshu is the man who opens his mouth and shows his heart.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 135

page_content: I doubt if this monk really saw Joshu’s heart.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 135

page_content: I hope he did not mistake the bell for a pitcher.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 135

page_content: It is too clear and so it is hard to see.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 135

page_content: A dunce once searched for a fire with a lighted lantern.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. Joshu Washes the Bowl, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 135

page_content: Had he known what fire was, He could have cooked his rice much sooner.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 136

page_content: Getsuan said to his students: “Keichu, the first wheel-maker of China, made two wheels of fifty spokes each.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 136

page_content: Now, suppose you removed the nave uniting the spokes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 136

page_content: What would become of the wheel?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 136

page_content: And had Keichu done this, could he be called the master wheel-maker?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 136

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If anyone can answer this question instantly, his eyes will be like a comet and his mind like a flash of lightning.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 136

page_content: When the hubless wheel turns, Master or no master can stop it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Keichu’s Wheel, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 136

page_content: It turns above heaven and below earth, South, north, east, and west.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: A monk asked Seijo: “I understand that a Buddha who lived before recorded history sat in meditation for ten cycles of existence and could not realize the highest truth, and so could not become fully emancipated.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 137

page_content: Why was this so?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: Seijo replied: “Your question is self-explanatory.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: The monk asked: “Since the Buddha was meditating, why could he not fulfill Buddhahood?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: Seijo said: “He was not a Buddha.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: Mumon’s comment: I will allow his realization, but I will not admit his understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 137

page_content: When one ignorant attains realization he is a saint.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 137

page_content: When a saint begins to understand he is ignorant.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 137

page_content: It is better to realize mind than body.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 137

page_content: When mind is realized one need not worry about body.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 137

page_content: When mind and body become one The man is free.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 9. A Buddha before History, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 137

page_content: Then he desires no praising.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: A monk named Seizei asked of Sozan: “Seizei is alone and poor.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 138

page_content: Will you give him support?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: Sozan asked: “Seizei?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: Seizei responded: “Yes, sir.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: Sozan said: “You have Zen, the best wine in China, and already have finished three cups, and still you are saying that they did not even wet your lips.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Seizei overplayed his hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 138

page_content: Why was it so?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 138

page_content: Because Sozan had eyes and knew with whom to deal.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 138

page_content: Even so, I want to ask: At what point did Seizei drink wine?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10. Seizei Alone and Poor, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 138

page_content: The poorest man in China, The bravest man in China, He barely sustains himself, Yet wishes to rival the wealthiest.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: Joshu went to a place where a monk had retired to meditate and asked him: “What is, is what?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: The monk raised his fist.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: Joshu replied: “Ships cannot remain where the water is too shallow.” And he left.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: A few days later Joshu went again to visit the monk and asked the same question.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: The monk answered the same way.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: Joshu said: “Well given, well taken, well killed, well saved.” And he bowed to the monk.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: Mumon’s comment: The raised fist was the same both times.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 139

page_content: Why is it Joshu did not admit the first and approved the second one?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 139

page_content: Where is the fault?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: Whoever answers this knows that Joshu’s tongue has no bone so he can use it freely.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 139

page_content: Yet perhaps Joshu is wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 139

page_content: Or, through that monk, he may have discovered his mistake.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: If anyone thinks that the one’s insight exceeds the other’s, he has no eyes.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 139

page_content: The light of the eyes is as a comet, And Zen’s activity is as lightning.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 11. Joshu Examines a Monk in Meditation, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 139

page_content: The sword that kills the man Is the sword that saves the man.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: Zuigan called out to himself every day: “Master.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: Then he answered himself: “Yes, sir.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: And after that he added: “Become sober.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: Again he answered: “Yes, sir.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: “And after that,” he continued, “do not be deceived by others.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: “Yes, sir; yes, sir,” he answered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Old Zuigan sells out and buys himself.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 140

page_content: He is opening a puppet show.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 140

page_content: He uses one mask to call “Master” and another that answers the master.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 140

page_content: Another mask says “Sober up” and another, “Do not be cheated by others.” If anyone clings to any of his masks, he is mistaken, yet if he imitates Zuigan, he will make himself fox-like.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 140

page_content: Some Zen students do not realize the true man in a mask Because they recognize ego-soul.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 12. Zuigan Calls His Own Master, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 140

page_content: Ego-soul is the seed of birth and death, And foolish people call it the true man.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: Tokusan went to the dining room from the meditation hall holding his bowl.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: Seppo was on duty cooking.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 141

page_content: When he met Tokusan he said: “The dinner drum is not yet beaten.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 141

page_content: Where are you going with your bowl?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: So Tokusan returned to his room.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: Seppo told Ganto about this.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: Ganto said: “Old Tokusan did not understand ultimate truth.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: Tokusan heard of this remark and asked Ganto to come to him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: “I have heard,” he said, “you are not approving my Zen.” Ganto admitted this indirectly.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 141

page_content: Tokusan said nothing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: The next day Tokusan delivered an entirely different kind of lecture to the monks.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: Ganto laughed and clapped his hands, saying: “I see our old man understands ultimate truth indeed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 141

page_content: None in China can surpass him.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Speaking about ultimate truth, both Ganto and Tokusan did not even dream it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: After all, they are dummies.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 141

page_content: Whoever understands the first truth Should understand the ultimate truth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 13. Tokusan Holds His Bowl, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 141

page_content: The last and first, Are they not the same?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: Nansen saw the monks of the eastern and western halls fighting over a cat.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 142

page_content: He seized the cat and told the monks: “If any of you say a good word, you can save the cat.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: No one answered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 142

page_content: So Nansen boldly cut the cat in two pieces.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: That evening Joshu returned and Nansen told him about this.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 142

page_content: Joshu removed his sandals and, placing them on his head, walked out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: Nansen said: “If you had been there, you could have saved the cat.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Why did Joshu put his sandals on his head?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 142

page_content: If anyone answers this question, he will understand exactly how Nansen enforced the edict.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 142

page_content: If not, he should watch his own head.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 142

page_content: Had Joshu been there, He would have enforced the edict oppositely.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 14. Nansen Cuts the Cat in Two, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 142

page_content: Joshu snatches the sword And Nansen begs for his life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Tozan went to Ummon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: Ummon asked him where he had come from.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Tozan said: “From Sato village.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Ummon asked: “In what temple did you remain for the summer?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Tozan replied: “The temple of Hoji, south of the lake.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: “When did you leave there?” asked Ummon, wondering how long Tozan would continue with such factual answers.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: “The twenty-fifth of August,” answered Tozan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Ummon said: “I should give you three blows with a stick, but today I forgive you.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: The next day Tozan bowed to Ummon and asked: “Yesterday you forgave me three blows.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: I do not know why you thought me wrong.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Ummon, rebuking Tozan’s spiritless responses, said: “You are good for nothing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: You simply wander from one monastery to another.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Before Ummon’s words were ended Tozan was enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Ummon fed Tozan good Zen food.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: If Tozan can digest it, Ummon may add another member to his family.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: In the evening Tozan swam around in a sea of good and bad, but at dawn Ummon crushed his nut shell.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: After all, he wasn’t so smart.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: Now, I want to ask: Did Tozan deserve the three blows?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: If you say yes, not only Tozan but every one of you deserves them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 13, sentence number 3 on page 143

page_content: If you say no, Ummon is speaking a lie.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 13, sentence number 4 on page 143

page_content: If you answer this question clearly, you can eat the same food as Tozan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 14, sentence number 1 on page 143

page_content: The lioness teaches her cubs roughly; The cubs jump and she knocks them down.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 15. Tozan’s Three Blows, paragraph number 14, sentence number 2 on page 143

page_content: When Ummon saw Tozan his first arrow was light; His second arrow shot deep.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 144

page_content: Ummon asked: “The world is such a wide world, why do you answer a bell and don ceremonial robes?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 144

page_content: Mumon’s comment: When one studies Zen one need not follow sound or color or form.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 144

page_content: Even though some have attained insight when hearing a voice or seeing a color or a form, this is a very common way.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 144

page_content: It is not true Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 144

page_content: The real Zen student controls sound, color, form, and actualizes the truth in his everyday life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 144

page_content: Sound comes to the ear, the ear goes to sound.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 144

page_content: When you blot out sound and sense, what do you understand?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 144

page_content: While listening with ears one never can understand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 144

page_content: To understand intimately one should see sound.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 144

page_content: When you understand, you belong to the family; When you do not understand, you are a stranger.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 16. Bells and Robes, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 144

page_content: Those who do not understand belong to the family, And when they understand they are strangers.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Chu, called Kokushi, the teacher of the emperor, called to his attendant: “Oshin.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Oshin answered: “Yes.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Chu repeated, to test his pupil: “Oshin.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Oshin repeated: “Yes.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Chu called: “Oshin.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Oshin answered: “Yes.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Chu said: “I ought to apologize to you for all this calling, but really you ought to apologize to me.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Mumon’s comment: When old Chu called Oshin three times his tongue was rotting, but when Oshin answered three times his words were brilliant.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 145

page_content: Chu was getting decrepit and lonesome, and his method of teaching was like holding a cow’s head to feed it clover.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Oshin did not trouble to show his Zen either.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 145

page_content: His satisfied stomach had no desire to feast.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 9, sentence number 3 on page 145

page_content: When the country is prosperous everyone is indolent; when the home is wealthy the children are spoiled.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: Now I want to ask you: Which one should apologize?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: When prison stocks are iron and have no place for the head, the

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: prisoner is doubly in trouble.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 12, sentence number 2 on page 145

page_content: When there is no place for Zen in the head of our generation, it is in

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 145

page_content: grievous trouble.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 17. The Three Calls of the Emperor’s Teacher, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 145

page_content: If you try to hold up the gate and door of a falling house, You also will be in trouble.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 146

page_content: A monk asked Tozan when he was weighing some flax: “What is Buddha?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 146

page_content: Tozan said: “This flax weighs three pounds.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 146

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Old Tozan’s Zen is like a clam.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 146

page_content: The minute the shell opens you see the whole inside.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 146

page_content: However, I want to ask you: Do you see the real Tozan?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 146

page_content: Three pounds of flax in front of your nose, Close enough, and mind is still closer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 18. Tozan’s Three Pounds, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 146

page_content: Whoever talks about affirmation and negation Lives in the right and wrong region.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Joshu asked Nansen: “What is the path?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Nansen said: “Everyday life is the path.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Joshu asked: “Can it be studied?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Nansen said: “If you try to study, you will be far away from it.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Joshu asked: “If I do not study, how can I know it is the path?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Nansen said: “The path does not belong to the perception world, neither does it belong to the nonperception world.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 147

page_content: Cognition is a delusion and noncognition is senseless.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 147

page_content: If you want to reach the true path beyond doubt, place yourself in the same freedom as sky.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 6, sentence number 4 on page 147

page_content: You name it neither good nor not-good.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: At these words Joshu was enlightened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Nansen could melt Joshu’s frozen doubts at once when Joshu asked his questions.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 147

page_content: I doubt though if Joshu reached the point that Nansen did.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 147

page_content: He needed thirty more years of study.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 147

page_content: In spring, hundreds of flowers; in autumn, a harvest moon; In summer, a refreshing breeze; in winter, snow will accompany you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 19. Everyday Life Is the Path, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 147

page_content: If useless things do not hang in your mind, Any season is a good season for you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. The Enlightened Man, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 148

page_content: Shogen asked: “Why does the enlightened man not stand on his feet and explain himself?” And he also said: “It is not necessary for speech to come from the tongue.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. The Enlightened Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 148

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Shogen spoke plainly enough, but how many will understand?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. The Enlightened Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 148

page_content: If anyone comprehends, he should come to my place and test out my big stick.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. The Enlightened Man, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 148

page_content: Why, look here, to test real gold you must see it through fire.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. The Enlightened Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 148

page_content: If the feet of enlightenment moved, the great ocean would overflow; If that head bowed, it would look down upon the heavens.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 20. The Enlightened Man, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 148

page_content: Such a body has no place to rest… Let another continue this poem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 149

page_content: A monk asked Ummon: “What is Buddha?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 149

page_content: Ummon answered him: “Dried dung.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 149

page_content: Mumon’s comment: It seems to me Ummon is so poor he cannot distinguish the taste of one food from another, or else he is too busy to write readable letters.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 149

page_content: Well, he tried to hold his school with dried dung.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 149

page_content: And his teaching was just as useless.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 149

page_content: Lightning flashes, Sparks shower.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 21. Dried Dung, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 149

page_content: In one blink of your eyes You have missed seeing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 150

page_content: Ananda asked Kashapa: “Buddha gave you the golden-woven robe of successorship.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 150

page_content: What else did he give you?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 150

page_content: Kashapa said: “Ananda.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 150

page_content: Ananda answered: “Yes, brother.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 150

page_content: Said Kashapa: “Now you can take down my preaching sign and put up your own.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 150

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If one understands this, he will see the old brotherhood still gathering, but if not, even though he has studied the truth from ages before the Buddhas, he will not attain enlightenment.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 150

page_content: The point of the question is dull but the answer is intimate.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 150

page_content: How many persons hearing it will open their eyes?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 22. Kashapa’s Preaching Sign, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 150

page_content: Elder brother calls and younger brother answers, This spring does not belong to the ordinary season.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: When he became emancipated the sixth patriarch received from the fifth patriarch the bowl and robe given from the Buddha to his successors, generation after generation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: A monk named E-myo out of envy pursued the patriarch to take this great treasure away from him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: The sixth patriarch placed the bowl and robe on a stone in the road and told E-myo: “These objects just symbolize the faith.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 151

page_content: There is no use fighting over them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 151

page_content: If you desire to take them, take them now.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: When E-myo went to move the bowl and robe they were as heavy as mountains.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: He could not budge them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 151

page_content: Trembling for shame he said: “I came wanting the teaching, not the material treasures.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 151

page_content: Please teach me.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: The sixth patriarch said: “When you do not think good and when you do not think not-good, what is your true self?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: At these words E-myo was illumined.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: Perspiration broke out all over his body.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 151

page_content: He cried and bowed, saying: “You have given me the secret words and meanings.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 151

page_content: Is there yet a deeper part of the teaching?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: The sixth patriarch replied: “What I have told you is no secret at all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: When you realize your own true self the secret belongs to you.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: E-myo said: “I was under the fifth patriarch many years but could not realize my true self until now.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: Through your teaching I find the source.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 151

page_content: A person drinks water and knows himself whether it is cold or warm.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 151

page_content: May I call you my teacher?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: The sixth patriarch replied: “We studied together under the fifth patriarch.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: Call him your teacher, but just treasure what you have attained.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: Mumon’s comment: The sixth patriarch certainly was kind in such an emergency.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 9, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: It was as if he removed the skin and seeds from the fruit and then, opening the pupil’s mouth, let him eat.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 151

page_content: You cannot describe it, you cannot picture it, You cannot admire it, you cannot sense it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 151

page_content: It is your true self, it has nowhere to hide.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 23. Do Not Think Good, Do Not Think Not-Good, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 152

page_content: When the world is destroyed, it will not be destroyed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 153

page_content: A monk asked Fuketsu: “Without speaking, without silence, how can you express the truth?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 153

page_content: Fuketsu observed: “I always remember springtime in southern China.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 153

page_content: The birds sing among innumerable kinds of fragrant flowers.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 153

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Fuketsu used to have lightning Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 153

page_content: Whenever he had the opportunity, he flashed it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 153

page_content: But this time he failed to do so and only borrowed from an old Chinese poem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 153

page_content: Never mind Fuketsu’s Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 3, sentence number 5 on page 153

page_content: If you want to express the truth, throw out your words, throw out your silence, and tell me about your own Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 153

page_content: Without revealing his own penetration, He offered another’s words, not his to give.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 24. Without Words, Without Silence, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 153
