page_content: Had he chattered on and on, Even his listeners would have been embarrassed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 154

page_content: In a dream Kyozan went to Maitreya’s Pure Land.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 154

page_content: He recognized himself seated in the third seat in the abode of Maitreya.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 154

page_content: Someone announced: “Today the one who sits in the third seat will preach.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 154

page_content: Kyozan arose and, hitting the gavel, said: “The truth of Mahayana teaching is transcendent, above words and thought.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 154

page_content: Do you understand?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 154

page_content: Mumon’s comment: I want to ask you monks: Did he preach or did he not?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 154

page_content: When he opens his mouth he is lost.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 154

page_content: When he seals his mouth he is lost.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 154

page_content: If he does not open it, if he does not seal it, he is 108,000 miles from truth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 154

page_content: In the light of day, Yet in a dream he talks of a dream.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 25. Preaching from the Third Seat, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 154

page_content: A monster among monsters, He intended to deceive the whole crowd.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 155

page_content: Hogen of Seiryo monastery was about to lecture before dinner when he noticed that the bamboo screen lowered for meditation had not been rolled up.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 155

page_content: He pointed to it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 155

page_content: Two monks arose from the audience and rolled it up.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 155

page_content: Hogen, observing the physical moment, said: “The state of the first monk is good, not that of the other.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 155

page_content: Mumon’s comment: I want to ask you: Which of those two monks gained and which lost?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 155

page_content: If any of you has one eye, he will see the failure on the teacher’s part.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 155

page_content: However, I am not discussing gain and loss.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 155

page_content: When the screen is rolled up the great sky opens, Yet the sky is not attuned to Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 26. Two Monks Roll Up the Screen, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 155

page_content: It is best to forget the great sky And to retire from every wind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 156

page_content: A monk asked Nansen: “Is there a teaching no master ever preached before?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 156

page_content: Nansen said: “Yes, there is.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 156

page_content: “What is it?” asked the monk.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 156

page_content: Nansen replied: “It is not mind, it is not Buddha, it is not things.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 156

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Old Nansen gave away his treasure-words.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 156

page_content: He must have been greatly upset.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 156

page_content: Nansen was too kind and lost his treasure.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 156

page_content: Truly, words have no power.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 27. It Is Not Mind, It Is Not Buddha, It Is Not Things, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 156

page_content: Even though the mountain becomes the sea, Words cannot open another’s mind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: Tokusan was studying Zen under Ryutan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 157

page_content: One night he came to Ryutan and asked many questions.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 157

page_content: The teacher said: “The night is getting old.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 157

page_content: Why don’t you retire?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: So Tokusan bowed and opened the screen to go out, observing: “It is very dark outside.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: Ryutan offered Tokusan a lighted candle to find his way.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 157

page_content: Just as Tokusan received it, Ryutan blew it out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 157

page_content: At that moment the mind of Tokusan was opened.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: “What have you attained?” asked Ryutan.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 157

page_content: “From now on,” said Tokusan, “I will not doubt the teacher’s words.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: The next day Ryutan told the monks at his lecture: “I see one monk among you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 157

page_content: His teeth are like the sword tree, his mouth is like the blood bowl.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 157

page_content: If you hit him hard with a big stick, he will not even so much as look back at you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 157

page_content: Someday he will mount the highest peak and carry my teaching there.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: On that day, in front of the lecture hall, Tokusan burned to ashes his commentaries on the sutras.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 157

page_content: He said: “However abstruse the teachings are, in comparison with this enlightenment they are like a single hair to the great sky.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 157

page_content: However profound the complicated knowledge of the world, compared to this enlightenment it is like one drop of water to the great ocean.” Then he left that monastery.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: Mumon’s comment: When Tokusan was in his own country he was not satisfied with Zen although he had heard about it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 157

page_content: He thought: “Those Southern monks say they can teach Dharma outside of the sutras.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 3 on page 157

page_content: They are all wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 4 on page 157

page_content: I must teach them.” So he traveled south.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 5 on page 157

page_content: He happened to stop near Ryutan’s monastery for refreshments.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 7, sentence number 6 on page 157

page_content: An old woman who was there asked him: “What are you carrying so heavily?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: Tokusan replied: “This is a commentary I have made on the Diamond Sutra after many years of work.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 9, sentence number 1 on page 157

page_content: The old woman said: “I read that sutra which says: ‘The past mind cannot be held, the present mind cannot be held, the future mind cannot be held.’ You wish some tea and refreshments.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 158

page_content: Which mind do you propose to use for them?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 10, sentence number 1 on page 158

page_content: Tokusan was as though dumb.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 10, sentence number 2 on page 158

page_content: Finally he asked the woman: “Do you know of any good teacher around here?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 11, sentence number 1 on page 158

page_content: The old woman referred him to Ryutan, not more than five miles away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 11, sentence number 2 on page 158

page_content: So he went to Ryutan in all humility, quite different from when he had started his journey.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 11, sentence number 3 on page 158

page_content: Ryutan in turn was so kind he forgot his own dignity.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 11, sentence number 4 on page 158

page_content: It was like pouring muddy water over a drunken man to sober him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 11, sentence number 5 on page 158

page_content: After all, it was an unnecessary comedy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 12, sentence number 1 on page 158

page_content: A hundred hearings cannot surpass one seeing, But after you see the teacher, that one glance cannot surpass a

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 13, sentence number 1 on page 158

page_content: hundred hearings.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 28. Blow Out the Candle, paragraph number 13, sentence number 2 on page 158

page_content: His nose was very high But he was blind after all.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 159

page_content: Two monks were arguing about a flag.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 159

page_content: One said: “The flag is moving.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 159

page_content: The other said: “The wind is moving.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 159

page_content: The sixth patriarch happened to be passing by.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 159

page_content: He told them: “Not the wind, not the flag; mind is moving.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 159

page_content: Mumon’s comment: The sixth patriarch said: “The wind is not moving, the flag is not moving.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 159

page_content: Mind is moving.” What did he mean?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 159

page_content: If you understand this intimately, you will see the two monks there trying to buy iron and gaining gold.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 159

page_content: The sixth patriarch could not bear to see those two dull heads, so he made such a bargain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 159

page_content: Wind, flag, mind moves, The same understanding.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 29. Not the Wind, Not the Flag, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 159

page_content: When the mouth opens All are wrong.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 160

page_content: Daibai asked Baso: “What is Buddha?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 160

page_content: Baso said: “This mind is Buddha.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 160

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If anyone wholly understands this, he is wearing Buddha’s clothing, he is eating Buddha’s food, he is speaking Buddha’s words, he is behaving as Buddha, he is Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 160

page_content: This anecdote, however, has given many a pupil the sickness of formality.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 160

page_content: If one truly understands, he will wash out his mouth for three days after saying the word Buddha, and he will close his ears and flee after hearing “This mind is Buddha.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 160

page_content: Under blue sky, in bright sunlight, One need not search around.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 30. This Mind Is Buddha, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 160

page_content: Asking what Buddha is Is like hiding loot in one’s pocket and declaring oneself innocent.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 161

page_content: A traveling monk asked an old woman the road to Taizan, a popular temple supposed to give wisdom to the one who worships there.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 161

page_content: The old woman said: “Go straight ahead.” When the monk proceeded a few steps, she said to herself: “He also is a common church-goer.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 161

page_content: Someone told this incident to Joshu, who said: “Wait until I investigate.” The next day he went and asked the same question, and the old woman gave the same answer.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 161

page_content: Joshu remarked: “I have investigated that old woman.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 161

page_content: Mumon’s comment: The old woman understood how war is planned, but she did not know how spies sneak in behind her tent.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 161

page_content: Old Joshu played the spy’s work and turned the tables on her, but he was not an able general.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 161

page_content: Both had their faults.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 161

page_content: Now I want to ask you: What was the point of Joshu’s investigating the old woman?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 161

page_content: When the question is common The answer is also common.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 31. Joshu Investigates, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 161

page_content: When the question is sand in a bowl of boiled rice The answer is a stick in the soft mud.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: A philosopher asked Buddha: “Without words, without the wordless, will you tell me truth?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: The Buddha kept silence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: The philosopher bowed and thanked the Buddha, saying: “With your loving kindness I have cleared away my delusions and entered the true path.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: After the philosopher had gone, Ananda asked the Buddha what he had attained.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: The Buddha replied: “A good horse runs even at the shadow of the whip.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Ananda was the disciple of the Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 162

page_content: Even so, his opinion did not surpass that of outsiders.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 162

page_content: I want to ask you monks: How much difference is there between disciples and outsiders?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 162

page_content: To tread the sharp edge of a sword, To run on smooth-frozen ice, One needs no footsteps to follow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 32. A Philosopher Asks Buddha, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 162

page_content: Walk over the cliffs with hands free.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 163

page_content: A monk asked Baso: “What is Buddha?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 163

page_content: Baso said: “This mind is not Buddha.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 163

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If anyone understands this, he is a graduate of Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 163

page_content: If you meet a fencing-master on the road, you may give him your

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 163

page_content: sword, If you meet a poet, you may offer him your poem.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 163

page_content: When you meet others, say only a part of what you intend.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 33. This Mind Is Not Buddha, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 163

page_content: Never give the whole thing at once.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 164

page_content: Nansen said: “Mind is not Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 164

page_content: Learning is not the path.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 164

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Nansen was getting old and forgot to be ashamed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 164

page_content: He spoke out with bad breath and exposed the scandal of his own home.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 164

page_content: However, there are few who appreciate his kindness.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 164

page_content: When the sky is clear the sun appears, When the earth is parched rain will fall.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 34. Learning Is Not the Path, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 164

page_content: He opened his heart fully and spoke out, But it was useless to talk to pigs and fish.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 165

page_content: “Seijo, the Chinese girl,” observed Goso, “had two souls, one always sick at home and the other in the city, a married woman with two children.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 165

page_content: Which was the true soul?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 165

page_content: Mumon’s comment: When one understands this, he will know it is possible to come out from one shell and enter another, as if one were stopping at a transient lodging house.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 165

page_content: But if he cannot understand, when his time comes and his four elements separate, he will be just like a crab dipped in boiling water, struggling with many hands and legs.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 165

page_content: In such a predicament he may say: “Mumon did not tell me where to go!” but it will be too late then.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 165

page_content: The moon above the clouds is the same moon, The mountains and rivers below are all different.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 165

page_content: Each is happy in its unity and variety.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 35. Two Souls, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 165

page_content: This is one, this is two.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Meeting a Zen Master on the Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 166

page_content: Goso said: “When you meet a Zen master on the road you cannot talk to him, you cannot face him with silence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Meeting a Zen Master on the Road, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 166

page_content: What are you going to do?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Meeting a Zen Master on the Road, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 166

page_content: Mumon’s comment: In such a case, if you can answer him intimately, your realization will be beautiful, but if you cannot, you should look about without seeing anything.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Meeting a Zen Master on the Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 166

page_content: Meeting a Zen master on the road, Face him neither with words nor silence.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 36. Meeting a Zen Master on the Road, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 166

page_content: Give him an uppercut And you will be called one who understands Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. A Buffalo Passes Through the Enclosure, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 167

page_content: Goso said: “When a buffalo goes out of his enclosure to the edge of the abyss, his horns and his head and his hoofs all pass through, but why can’t the tail also pass?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. A Buffalo Passes Through the Enclosure, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 167

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If anyone can open one eye at this point and say a word of Zen, he is qualified to repay the four gratifications, and, not only that, he can save all sentient beings under him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. A Buffalo Passes Through the Enclosure, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 167

page_content: But if he cannot say such a word of true Zen, he should turn back to his tail.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. A Buffalo Passes Through the Enclosure, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 167

page_content: If the buffalo runs, he will fall into the trench; If he returns, he will be butchered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 37. A Buffalo Passes Through the Enclosure, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 167

page_content: That little tail Is a very strange thing.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 168

page_content: A monk asked Joshu why Bodhidharma came to China.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 168

page_content: Joshu said: “An oak tree in the garden.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 168

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If one sees Joshu’s answer clearly, there is no Shakyamuni Buddha before him and no future Buddha after him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 168

page_content: Words cannot describe everything.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 168

page_content: The heart’s message cannot be delivered in words.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 168

page_content: If one receives words literally, he will be lost, If he tries to explain with words, he will not attain enlightenment in

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 38. An Oak Tree in the Garden, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 168

page_content: this life.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: A Zen student told Ummon: “Brilliancy of Buddha illuminates the whole universe.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: Before he finished the phrase Ummon asked: “You are reciting another’s poem, are you not?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: “Yes,” answered the student.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: “You are sidetracked,” said Ummon.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: Afterwards another teacher, Shishin, asked his pupils: “At what point did that student go off the track?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If anyone perceives Ummon’s particular skillfulness, he will know at what point the student was off the track, and he will be a teacher of man and Devas.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 169

page_content: If not, he cannot even perceive himself.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 169

page_content: When a fish meets the fishhook If he is too greedy, he will be caught.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 39. Ummon’s Sidetrack, paragraph number 7, sentence number 2 on page 169

page_content: When his mouth opens His life already is lost.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 170

page_content: Hyakujo wished to send a monk to open a new monastery.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 170

page_content: He told his pupils that whoever answered a question most ably would be appointed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 170

page_content: Placing a water vase on the ground, he asked: “Who can say what this is without calling its name?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 170

page_content: The chief monk said: “No one can call it a wooden shoe.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 170

page_content: Isan, the cooking monk, tipped over the vase with his foot and went out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 170

page_content: Hyakujo smiled and said: “The chief monk loses.” And Isan became the master of the new monastery.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 170

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Isan was brave enough, but he could not escape Hyakujo’s trick.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 170

page_content: After all, he gave up a light job and took a heavy one.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 170

page_content: Why, can’t you see, he took off his comfortable hat and placed himself in iron stocks.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 40. Tipping Over a Water Vase, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 170

page_content: Giving up cooking utensils, Defeating the chatterbox, Though his teacher sets a barrier for him His feet will tip over everything, even the Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 171

page_content: Bodhidharma sits facing the wall.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 171

page_content: His future successor stands in the snow and presents his severed arm to Bodhidharma.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 171

page_content: He cries: “My mind is not pacified.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 171

page_content: Master, pacify my mind.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 171

page_content: Bodhidharma says: “If you bring me that mind, I will pacify it for you.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 171

page_content: The successor says: “When I search my mind I cannot hold it.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 171

page_content: Bodhidharma says: “Then your mind is pacified already.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 171

page_content: Mumon’s comment: That broken-toothed old Hindu, Bodhidharma, came thousands of miles over the sea from India to China as if he had something wonderful.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 171

page_content: He is like raising waves without wind.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 171

page_content: After he remained years in China he had only one disciple and that one lost his arm and was deformed.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 171

page_content: Alas, ever since he has had brainless disciples.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 171

page_content: Why did Bodhidharma come to China?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 171

page_content: For years monks have discussed this.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 41. Bodhidharma Pacifies the Mind, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 171

page_content: All the troubles that have followed since Came from that teacher and disciple.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: In the time of Buddha Shakyamuni, Manjusri went to the assemblage of the Buddhas.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: When he arrived there, the conference was over and each Buddha had returned to his own Buddha-land.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 172

page_content: Only one girl was yet unmoved in deep meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: Manjusri asked Buddha Shakyamuni how it was possible for this girl to reach this state, one which even he could not attain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: “Bring her out from Samadhi and ask her yourself,” said the Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: Manjusri walked around the girl three times and snapped his fingers.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: She still remained in meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 172

page_content: So by his miracle power he transported her to a high heaven and tried his best to call her, but in vain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: Buddha Shakyamuni said: “Even a hundred thousand Manjusris could not disturb her, but below this place, past twelve hundred million countries, is a Bodhisattva, Mo-myo, seed of delusion.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: If he comes here, she will awaken.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: No sooner had the Buddha spoken than that Bodhisattva sprang up from the earth and bowed and paid homage to the Buddha.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: Buddha directed him to arouse the girl.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 172

page_content: The Bodhisattva went in front of the girl and snapped his fingers, and in that instant the girl came out from her deep meditation.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Old Shakyamuni set a very poor stage.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: I want to ask you monks: If Manjusri, who is supposed to have been the teacher of seven Buddhas, could not bring this girl out of meditation, how then could a Bodhisattva who was a mere beginner?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 7, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: If you understand this intimately, you yourself can enter the great meditation while you are living in the world of delusion.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 1 on page 172

page_content: One could not awaken her, the other could.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 2 on page 172

page_content: Neither are good actors.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 3 on page 172

page_content: One wears the mask of god, one a devil’s mask.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 42. The Girl Comes Out from Meditation, paragraph number 8, sentence number 4 on page 172

page_content: Had both failed, the drama still would be a comedy.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 173

page_content: Shuzan held out his short staff and said: “If you call this a short staff, you oppose its reality.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 173

page_content: If you do not call it a short staff, you ignore the fact.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 173

page_content: Now what do you wish to call this?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 173

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If you call this a short staff, you oppose its reality.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 173

page_content: If you do not call it a short staff, you ignore the fact.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 173

page_content: It cannot be expressed with words and it cannot be expressed without words.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 173

page_content: Now say quickly what it is.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 173

page_content: Holding out the short staff, He gave an order of life or death.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 43. Shuzan’s Short Staff, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 173

page_content: Positive and negative interwoven, Even Buddhas and patriarchs cannot escape this attack.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 174

page_content: Basho said to his disciple: “When you have a staff, I will give it to you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 174

page_content: If you have no staff, I will take it away from you.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 174

page_content: Mumon’s comment: When there is no bridge over the creek the staff will help me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 174

page_content: When I return home on a moonless night the staff will accompany me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 174

page_content: But if you call this a staff, you will enter hell like an arrow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 174

page_content: With this staff in my hand I can measure the depths and shallows of the world.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 174

page_content: The staff supports the heavens and makes firm the earth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 44. Basho’s Staff, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 174

page_content: Everywhere it goes the true teaching will be spread.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 175

page_content: Hoen said: “The past and future Buddhas, both are his servants.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 175

page_content: Who is he?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 175

page_content: Mumon’s comment: If you realize clearly who he is, it is as if you met your own father on a busy street.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 175

page_content: There is no need to ask anyone whether or not your recognition is true.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 175

page_content: Do not fight with another’s bow and arrow.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 175

page_content: Do not ride another’s horse.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 175

page_content: Do not discuss another’s faults.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 45. Who Is He?, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 175

page_content: Do not interfere with another’s work.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 176

page_content: Sekiso asked: “How can you proceed on from the top of a hundred-foot pole?” Another Zen teacher said: “One who sits on the top of a hundred- foot pole has attained a certain height but still is not handling Zen freely.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 176

page_content: He should proceed on from there and appear with his whole body in the ten parts of the world.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 176

page_content: Mumon’s comment: One can continue his steps or turn his body freely about on the top of the pole.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 176

page_content: In either case he should be respected.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 176

page_content: I want to ask you monks, however: How will you proceed from the top of that pole?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 176

page_content: Look out!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 176

page_content: The man who lacks the third eye of insight Will cling to the measure of the hundred feet.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 46. Proceed from the Top of the Pole, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 176

page_content: Such a man will jump from there and kill himself, Like a blind man misleading other blind men.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 177

page_content: Tosotsu built three barriers and made the monks pass through them.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 177

page_content: The first barrier is studying Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 177

page_content: In studying Zen the aim is to see one’s own true nature.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 177

page_content: Now where is your true nature?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 177

page_content: Secondly, when one realizes his own true nature he will be free from birth and death.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 177

page_content: Now when you shut the light from your eyes and become a corpse, how can you free yourself?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 177

page_content: Thirdly, if you free yourself from birth and death, you should know where you are.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 177

page_content: Now your body separates into the four elements.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 177

page_content: Where are you?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 177

page_content: Mumon’s comment: Whoever can pass these three barriers will be a master wherever he stands.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 177

page_content: Whatever happens about him he will turn into Zen.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 177

page_content: Otherwise he will be living on poor food and not even enough of that to satisfy himself.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 177

page_content: An instant realization sees endless time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 177

page_content: Endless time is as one moment.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 47. Three Gates of Tosotsu, paragraph number 6, sentence number 3 on page 177

page_content: When one comprehends the endless moment He realizes the person who is seeing it.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 178

page_content: A Zen pupil asked Kembo: “All Buddhas of the ten parts of the universe enter the one road of Nirvana.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 178

page_content: Where does that road begin?”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 178

page_content: Kembo, raising his walking stick and drawing the figure one in the air, said: “Here it is.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 178

page_content: This pupil went to Ummon and asked the same question.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 178

page_content: Ummon, who happened to have a fan in his hand, said: “This fan will reach to the thirty- third heaven and hit the nose of the presiding deity there.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 178

page_content: It is like the Dragon Carp of the Eastern Sea tipping over the rain-cloud with his tail.”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 178

page_content: Mumon’s comment: One teacher enters the deep sea and scratches the earth and raises dust.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 178

page_content: The other goes to the mountain top and raises waves that almost touch heaven.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 178

page_content: One holds, the other gives out.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 178

page_content: Each supports the profound teaching with a single hand.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 178

page_content: Kembo and Ummon are like two riders neither of whom can surpass the other.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 178

page_content: It is very difficult to find the perfect man.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 178

page_content: Frankly, neither of them know where the road starts.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 178

page_content: Before the first step is taken the goal is reached.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 178

page_content: Before the tongue is moved the speech is finished.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 48. One Road of Kembo, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 178

page_content: More than brilliant intuition is needed To find the origin of the right road.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 178

page_content: Amban, a layman Zen student, said: “Mumon has just published forty-eight koans and called the book Gateless Gate.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 178

page_content: He criticizes the old patriarchs’ words and actions.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 178

page_content: I think he is very mischievous.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 178

page_content: He is like an old doughnut seller trying to catch a passerby to force his doughnuts down his mouth.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 178

page_content: The customer can neither swallow nor spit out the doughnuts, and this causes suffering.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 6 on page 178

page_content: Mumon has annoyed everyone enough, so I think I shall add one more as a bargain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 1, sentence number 7 on page 178

page_content: I wonder if he himself can eat this bargain.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 179

page_content: If he can, and digest it well, it will be fine, but if not, we will have to put it back into the frying pan with his forty-eight also and cook them again.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 179

page_content: Mumon, you eat first, before someone else does:

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 179

page_content: “Buddha, according to a sutra, once said: ‘Stop, stop.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 179

page_content: Do not speak.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 179

page_content: The ultimate truth is not even to think.’”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 179

page_content: Amban’s comment: Where did that so-called teaching come from?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 179

page_content: How is it that one could not even think it?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 179

page_content: Suppose someone spoke about it then what became of it?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 179

page_content: Buddha himself was a great chatterbox and in this sutra spoke contrarily.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 179

page_content: Because of this, persons like Mumon appear afterwards in China and make useless doughnuts, annoying people.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 179

page_content: What shall we do after all?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 4, sentence number 7 on page 179

page_content: I will show you.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 5, sentence number 1 on page 179

page_content: Then Amban put his palms together, folded his hands, and said: “Stop, stop.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 5, sentence number 2 on page 179

page_content: Do not speak.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 5, sentence number 3 on page 179

page_content: The ultimate truth is not even to think.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 5, sentence number 4 on page 179

page_content: And now I will make a little circle on the sutra with my finger and add that five thousand other sutras and Vimalakirti’s gateless gate all are here!”

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 6, sentence number 1 on page 179

page_content: If anyone tells you fire is light, Pay no attention.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 49. Amban’s Addition, paragraph number 6, sentence number 2 on page 179

page_content: When two thieves meet they need no introduction: They recognize each other without question.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10 BULLS, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 181

page_content: by Kakuan

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10 BULLS, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 182

page_content: Transcribed by Nyogen Senzaki and Paul Reps

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 10 BULLS, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 183

page_content: Illustrated by Tomikichiro Tokuriki

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 184

page_content: In the pasture of this world, I endlessly push aside the tall grasses in

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 184

page_content: search of the bull.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 184

page_content: Following unnamed rivers, lost upon the interpenetrating paths of

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 184

page_content: distant mountains, My strength failing and my vitality exhausted, I cannot find the bull.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 184

page_content: I only hear the locusts chirring through the forest at night.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 4, sentence number 1 on page 184

page_content: Comment: The bull never has been lost.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 4, sentence number 2 on page 184

page_content: What need is there to search?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 4, sentence number 3 on page 184

page_content: Only because of separation from my true nature, I fail to find him.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 4, sentence number 4 on page 184

page_content: In the confusion of the senses I lose even his tracks.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 4, sentence number 5 on page 184

page_content: Far from home, I see many crossroads, but which way is the right one I know not.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 1. The Search for the Bull, paragraph number 4, sentence number 6 on page 184

page_content: Greed and fear, good and bad, entangle me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 185

page_content: Along the riverbank under the trees, I discover footprints!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 185

page_content: Even under the fragrant grass I see his prints.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 185

page_content: Deep in remote mountains they are found.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 185

page_content: These traces no more can be hidden than one’s nose, looking

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 185

page_content: heavenward.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 185

page_content: Comment: Understanding the teaching, I see the footprints of the bull.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 185

page_content: Then I learn that, just as many utensils are made from one metal, so too are myriad entities made of the fabric of self.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 185

page_content: Unless I discriminate, how will I perceive the true from the untrue?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 2. Discovering the Footprints, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 185

page_content: Not yet having entered the gate, nevertheless I have discerned the path.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 186

page_content: I hear the song of the nightingale.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 186

page_content: The sun is warm, the wind is mild, willows are green along the shore, Here no bull can hide!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 186

page_content: What artist can draw that massive head, those majestic horns?

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 186

page_content: Comment: When one hears the voice, one can sense its source.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 186

page_content: As soon as the six senses merge, the gate is entered.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 186

page_content: Wherever one enters one sees the head of the bull!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 186

page_content: This unity is like salt in water, like color in dyestuff.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 3. Perceiving the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 186

page_content: The slightest thing is not apart from self.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 187

page_content: I seize him with a terrific struggle.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 187

page_content: His great will and power are inexhaustible.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 187

page_content: He charges to the high plateau far above the cloud-mists, Or in an impenetrable ravine he stands.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 187

page_content: Comment: He dwelt in the forest a long time, but I caught him today!

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 187

page_content: Infatuation for scenery interferes with his direction.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 187

page_content: Longing for sweeter grass, he wanders away.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 187

page_content: His mind still is stubborn and unbridled.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 4. Catching the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 187

page_content: If I wish him to submit, I must raise my whip.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 188

page_content: The whip and rope are necessary, Else he might stray off down some dusty road.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 188

page_content: Being well trained, he becomes naturally gentle.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 188

page_content: Then, unfettered, he obeys his master.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 188

page_content: Comment: When one thought arises, another thought follows.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 188

page_content: When the first thought springs from enlightenment, all subsequent thoughts are true.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 188

page_content: Through delusion, one makes everything untrue.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 188

page_content: Delusion is not caused by objectivity; it is the result of subjectivity.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 5. Taming the Bull, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 188

page_content: Hold the nose-ring tight and do not allow even a doubt.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 189

page_content: Mounting the bull, slowly I return homeward.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 189

page_content: The voice of my flute intones through the evening.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 189

page_content: Measuring with hand-beats the pulsating harmony, I direct the endless

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 189

page_content: rhythm.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 189

page_content: Whoever hears this melody will join me.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 3, sentence number 1 on page 189

page_content: Comment: This struggle is over; gain and loss are assimilated.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 3, sentence number 2 on page 189

page_content: I sing the song of the village woodsman, and play the tunes of the children.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 3, sentence number 3 on page 189

page_content: Astride the bull, I observe the clouds above.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 6. Riding the Bull Home, paragraph number 3, sentence number 4 on page 189

page_content: Onward I go, no matter who may wish to call me back.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 190

page_content: Astride the bull, I reach home.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 1, sentence number 2 on page 190

page_content: I am serene.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 1, sentence number 3 on page 190

page_content: The bull too can rest.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 1, sentence number 4 on page 190

page_content: The dawn has come.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 1, sentence number 5 on page 190

page_content: In blissful repose, Within my thatched dwelling I have abandoned the whip and rope.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 2, sentence number 1 on page 190

page_content: Comment: All is one law, not two.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 2, sentence number 2 on page 190

page_content: We only make the bull a temporary subject.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 2, sentence number 3 on page 190

page_content: It is as the relation of rabbit and trap, of fish and net.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 2, sentence number 4 on page 190

page_content: It is as gold and dross, or the moon emerging from a cloud.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 7. The Bull Transcended, paragraph number 2, sentence number 5 on page 190

page_content: One path of clear light travels on throughout endless time.

metadata.source: Source document: ZEN FLESH, ZEN BONES, Chapter: 8. Both Bull and Self Transcended, paragraph number 1, sentence number 1 on page 191

page_content: Whip, rope, person, and bull — all merge in No-Thing.
